Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S381), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Conventional magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) techniques are highly sensitive to detect multiple sclerosis (MS) plaques, enabling a quantitative assessment of inflammatory activity and lesion load. In quantitative analyses of focal lesions, manual or semi-automated segmentations have been widely used to compute the total number of lesions and the total lesion volume. These techniques, however, are both challenging and time-consuming, being also prone to intra-observer and inter-observer variability. Aim: To develop an automated approach to segment brain tissues and MS lesions from brain MRI images. The goal is to reduce the user interaction and to provide an objective tool that eliminates the inter- and intra-observer variability. Methods: Based on the recent methods developed by Souplet et al. and de Boer et al., we propose a novel pipeline which includes the following steps: bias correction, skull stripping, atlas registration, tissue classification, and lesion segmentation. After the initial pre-processing steps, a MRI scan is automatically segmented into 4 classes: white matter (WM), grey matter (GM), cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and partial volume. An expectation maximisation method which fits a multivariate Gaussian mixture model to T1-w, T2-w and PD-w images is used for this purpose. Based on the obtained tissue masks and using the estimated GM mean and variance, we apply an intensity threshold to the FLAIR image, which provides the lesion segmentation. With the aim of improving this initial result, spatial information coming from the neighbouring tissue labels is used to refine the final lesion segmentation. Results:The experimental evaluation was performed using real data sets of 1.5T and the corresponding ground truth annotations provided by expert radiologists. The following values were obtained: 64% of true positive (TP) fraction, 80% of false positive (FP) fraction, and an average surface distance of 7.89 mm. The results of our approach were quantitatively compared to our implementations of the works of Souplet et al. and de Boer et al., obtaining higher TP and lower FP values. Conclusion: Promising MS lesion segmentation results have been obtained in terms of TP. However, the high number of FP which is still a well-known problem of all the automated MS lesion segmentation approaches has to be improved in order to use them for the standard clinical practice. Our future work will focus on tackling this issue.

Title (TI): 7T MRI reveals the perivascular distribution of lesions in an animal model of multiple sclerosis.
Comments: Gadolinium and multiple sclerosis: Vessels, barriers of the brain, and glymphatics.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S376), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background and Objective: Histopathology and MRI studies have demonstrated the perivascular distribution of most MS lesions. Studies of EAE in the common marmoset (Callithrix jacchus) have revealed striking similarities to human MS. We therefore sought to determine whether a perivascular topography of MS-like lesions can be detected in marmoset EAE by ultrahigh- field (7T) T2*-weighted imaging. Methods: An ex vivo study was performed in a 7T/30cm Bruker MRI scanner using formalin-fixed brains and spinal cords from a non immunized control marmoset, a marmoset immunized with recombinant human myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (rh- MOG; A1), and a marmoset immunized with whole white matter homogenate (WMH; A2). Monkeys were euthanized according to the guidelines of the ethics committee. Tissues were placed in a plastic tube filled with Fomblin and inserted into a single-channel radiofrequency coil. The MRI protocol included the following sequences: whole brain spin echo (0.1x 0.1mm2 in-plane resolution; slice thickness=0.3mm; TR=12000ms; TE=48ms for T2 weighting; AT=1hour), whole brain multi-gradient-echo (0.1mm3 isotropic voxels; TR=57ms; TE= 3, 7, 11, 15, 19, 23, 27, 31ms; FA=16deg; AT=1.1hour), and spinal cord gradient echo (0.1 mm3 isotropic voxels; TR=300ms; TE=5.23ms; FA=60deg, AT= 2hours). Each sequence was repeated 8 times and averaged. Results: A2 developed relapsing-remitting disease and was euthanized at month 7, whereas A1 had an acute onset, rapid progression and was euthanized at month 2.Imaging of the spine demonstrated that A2 had 13 lesions (diameter: 0.4+/-0.1mm), 92% of which featured a conspicuous central vessel, whereas A1 had 12 larger, confluent, peripheral lesions with no clear perivascular distribution. Brain imaging of A2 showed 61 multifocal microscopic lesions (0.35+/-0.1mm), 88% of which had a central vessel. A1 had disseminated and confluent large lesions with hemorrhagic areas and optic nerve involvement. Although vessels were seen within lesions, a clear central vein was not appreciated. The control animal had a normal MRI. Discussion: Ex vivo ultra-high-resolution MRI demonstrated heterogeneity in lesion types in the two EAE models induced in this study. The typical multifocal perivascular distribution described in MS lesions was observed in the WMH-EAE animal, which is more concordant with observations in MS.

Title (TI): Objective olfactory testing of patients with multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S314-S315), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Olfactory dysfunction is reported in several studies. We measured Olfactory Evoked Potentials (OEP), olfactory bulb (OB) and olfactory brain volumes of patients with multiple sclerosis (MS). Olfactory testing results were correlated with the number and volume of MS lesions in the olfactory brain. Materials and Methods: 34 MS patients (22-65 years,) and 6 patients with optic-neuritis (ON) in the beginning state of disease were included. 30 healthy control persons were tested. Psychophysical olfactory testing was performed using the Threshold-Discrimination-Identification test (TDI). OEPs were measured with an olfactometer. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) measurements were performed with manual segmentation. Results: 41% of 34 MS patients ,8% of 30 control persons and 83% of 6 patients with optic neuritis displayed olfactory dysfunction. OB volumes correlated with OEPs (r=0.56,p<0.01). There was a correlation between the OB volume and the number (r=-0.34, p<0.05) and volume (r=-0.37,p<0.05) of MS lesions in the olfactory brain. The volume of the left OB correlated with the number (r=-0.37, p<0.05) and volume (r=-0.4, p<0.05) of MS lesions in the left olfactory brain.There was a correlation between the olfactory brain volume and the volume of lesions in the olfactory brain (r=-0.33, p=0.05), the olfactory brain volume and the EDSS score (r=-0.37,p<0.05). The olfactory brain volumes of patients with primary and secondary progressive form were lesser than those with relapsing-remitting form (p=0.03). Conclusions: The correlation between OEPs and MRI measurements shows that OB and olfactory brain volume could provide information about the olfactory function in MS patients. Patients with ON showed more frequently olfactory dysfunction. OB and olfactory brain volumes correlated with the number and volume of MS lesions in the olfactory brain. Further studies are needed.

Title (TI): Parkinsoniansyndrome in multiple sclerosis: A report of 34 cases.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S304), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Objective: Describe clinical and radiological data in clinical defined Multiple Sclerosis (MS) patients presenting with Parkinsonian Syndrom (PS) from an observational retrospective study in order to analyse mechanisms of this association. Design/Methods: Data set were collected from 1995 through 2010 by 9 MS Universitary-hospital centers included in a MS national network (Club Francophone de la Sclerose en Plaques). A standard report, including data on PS (main symptoms, dopa sensitivity), on MS (type of disease, response to intravenous corticosteroids, EDSS at the time of PS diagnosis), MRI and DATScan findings was filled in by the same referent neurologist in each center. Patients were classified as: A) Coincidental association of Parkinson Diseases (PD) and MS (group 1) if they have 2 of 3 of following criteria: mean clinical symptoms of PD, DatScan positivity, dopa responsiveness (> 50%) b) Acute PS during MS relapse (group 2) if they fulfilled 2 of 3 of following criteria: Acute or subacute onset, improvement after corticosteroids, demyelinating lesion localized in basal ganglia including substantia nigra c) PS related to progressive MS (group 3) with disability, no improvement after dopatherapy or corticosteroids, normal DatScan Results: 34 cases were collected in this cohort. Median age at onset of PS was 50 years [26-64] which is younger than classical age onset of PD. 51% presented loss of postural reflexes with axial rigidity. The rest tremor is less frequent (71%) than in PD. Mean interval between onset of MS and PS is close to 10 years (mettre la moyenne excate +/- SD) . When PS occurred, observed EDSS was between 3 and 6 (60%). We observed 35% of unequivocal response to dopaminergic treatment and 21% of significant improvement after intravenous corticosteroids. A demyelinating lesion localized in the midbrain was demonstrated in 30% cases (9/30). DAT Scan was abnormal in 10/21(48%) cases performed. There are 11 cases in Group 1, 12 in group 2 and 11 in group 3. Conclusions/Relevance: Side to coincidental association of PD with MS (33%), PS was related to MS disease either during relapse or in a progressive clinical setting in disabled patients suggesting disturbance within the basal ganglia circuitry due to MS lesions.

Title (TI): Neuropsychological testing and "voxel-based" diffusion tensor analyses of patients with initial stages of multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S173), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Neuropsychological changes occurr in patients with multiple sclerosis (MS) quite common and have a great impact on quality of life. Many conventional magnetization resonance imaging (MRI) studies were done to assess structural changes in relation to cognitive dysfunction discovered only moderate correlations. Conventional MRI seems to have limited sensitivity for MS lesions leaving subtle white matter changes undetected. The aim of this study was to investigate a potential relationship between the subtle changes in the normal-appearing white matter and the development of neuropsychological changes in MS using non-conventional MRI technique "voxel-based" diffusion tensor analyses. Methods: In our cohort 14 patients, 8 males and 6 females were tested neuropsychogically and scanned by diffusion tensor imaging technique (DTI). 10 patients had clinically isolated syndrom (CIS), 3 patients had early relapsing remitting MS and 1 patient had newly diagnosed primary progressive MS. Concerning neuropsychological testing we used following tests: Wechsler intelligence and memory scales, Rey-Osterrieth Complex Figure, d2 Test of Attention, Stroop test, Verbal fluency test, Trail making test (TMT) and Zung's subjective depression scale. Tract based spatial statistics (TBSS) for the voxel-based analyses of DTI were used. Patients were compared with 14 age matched healthy volunteers. Results: Cognitive functions measured by our neuropsychological test batteries were normal in all 14 patients except in TMT, in which all patients showed slightly worse scores. Fractional anisotrophy (FA) values on a voxel-wise basis using TBSS were significantly lower (significance level p<0.15) in periventricular white matter, corpus callosum and both optic tracts was in patients in comparison with healthy subjects. Conclusion: Neuropsychiatric changes are described also in early stages of MS as well as CIS. An explanation of almost normal results of neuropsychological testing in patient's cohort is its relatively small size and possibility of still large compensatory reserves of nervous system. Significant changes in values of FA in specific white matter areas of MS patients using nonconventional MRI technique likely reflect changes which pre-date subsequent neuropsychological changes. Especially in corpus callosum, which is responsible for interhemisferic disconnection, may predict cognitive decline and support the concept of early treatment preventing irreversible damage.

Title (TI): A single 7T MRI brain scan accurately predicts eventual diagnosis of MS in cases with initial diagnostic uncertainty.
Comments: The use of multiparametric quantitative magnetic resonance imaging for evaluating visually assigned lesion groups in patients with multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S165-S166), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Conventional MRI depicts MS lesions in a nonspecific way. Enhancing MRI's specificity for MS could lessen rates of misdiagnosis, reduce additional paraclinical testing, and expedite diagnosis. In-vivo T2*-weighted 7 Tesla (7T) MRI can be used to visualise central veins in most MS lesions (Tallantyre et al. 2008), consistent with histopathology (Fog 1964). The percentage of lesions with a visible central vein reliably distinguishes patients known to have MS (in whom >40% lesions have central veins) from patients with microangiopathic white matter brain lesions (in whom <40% lesions have central veins)(Tallantyre et al. 2011). Objective: To prospectively assess the predictive value (for an eventual diagnosis of MS) of a single T2*-weighted 7T MRI scan, for patients that a Neurologist and Neuroradiologist could not diagnose without further paraclinical testing and clinical follow-up. Patients and methods: Twenty patients have been recruited so far, from the Neurology outpatients department at Queens Medical Centre, Nottingham, UK; comprising 16 females and 4 males of average age 49.5 years (range 32.1 to 75.4). They were undiagnosed despite assessment by a consultant Neurologist and interpretation of conventional hospital MRI scanning by a consultant Neuroradiologist. All subjects gave informed consent and the study had ethical approval from the Nottingham research ethics committee. Image acquisition and analysis (blinded to clinical data) was performed using methods previously described (Tallantyre et al. 2011). Patients continue under follow-up by their treating Neurologist for a current average of 1.3 years (range 0.7 to 2.0). Results: Of the 20 patients studied, 18 have a diagnosis from their Neurologist. All 13 patients with an eventual diagnosis of demyelinating disease had central veins visible in >40% of brain lesions (mean 91%, range 70% to 100%). All 5 patients with an eventual non- MS diagnosis had central veins visible in <40% of lesions (mean 18%, range 9% to 29%). No patient with positive CSF oligoclonal bands had <40% of lesions with central veins. Conclusion: In the current cohort T2*-weighted 7T MRI had 100% positive and negative predictive value for MS in patients with initial diagnostic doubt. Translation of this technique to clinically available 3T MRI scanners could overhaul future diagnostic algorithms.

Title (TI): Grey matter volume determination in clinically isolated syndromes and patients with multiple sclerosis: Role of lesion masking.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S162), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Objective: To assess the effect of using lesion masking in the determination of grey matter volume in patients with clinically isolated syndromes (CIS), relapsing-remitting (RR) MS, and primary progressive (PP) MS. Introduction: Volume estimation using automated techniques based on image segmentation is prone to error due to white matter hyperintensities (lesions) being erroneously segmented as grey matter and thus contributing to the estimated grey matter volume. Materials and methods: The study included patients with a CIS (n=216), RRMS (n=157) and PPMS (n=72). Images were acquired on a 1.5T MRI unit (Symphony, Siemens). Lesion masks were delineated semi-automatically over PD-T2-weighted images using DISPIMAGE software. T1-weighted images were segmented using Statistical Parametric Mapping 5 (SPM) and the spm-segment toolbox. Segmentation was performed twice, with and without lesion masking. For each patient, the percentage difference between the two estimated grey matter volumes was calculated as %error-gm=100*(gm-no-mask-gm-mask)/ gm-mask. In addition, a test-retest study was performed in a set of 4 patients. A significant change in grey matter volume determination resulting from inclusion of lesion masking was defined as a value more than twice the coefficient of variation of the test-retest measurements. Results: The mean (min, max) estimated error in grey matter volume was 0.21 (0.0, 1.98) in CIS patients, 0.54 (0.0, 3.60) in RR MS, and 1.50 (0.03, 6.78) in PP MS. Lesion volumes were 4.83 cc (0.04, 37.84) in CIS, 9.83 cc (0.13, 56.01) in RR MS and 27.00 cc (0.67, 164.0) in PP MS. The test-retest study yielded an error of 1.50 %. The error in grey matter volume in studies without masking was greater than the test-retest variation in 4/216 CIS patients, 13/157 RR MS and 27/72 PP MS. Conclusions: The effect of including lesion masking in grey matter volume estimation was significant in 2 % of CIS patients, 8 % of RR MS and 37 % of PP MS. The difference among groups is probably just related to the lesion load. Future work should analyze the role of lesion masking for grey matter volume determination in high-field magnets (3.0T). Another aspect to consider is the use of lesion-filling techniques.

Title (TI): Computer-assisted strategies to automated quantification of multiple sclerosis lesion evolution on brain magnetic resonance imaging.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S161-S162), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Aiming to reduce the intra and inter-observer variability, several automated approaches have been proposed with the goal of assessing the multiple sclerosis (MS) lesion evolution, including both lesion detection and lesion load change quantification. Aim: To study different methods used to perform the MS lesion evolution analysis on serial brain MRI. Methods: We analyzed more than 30 automated MS lesion change detection techniques with the aim of pointing out their advantages and drawbacks. These methods can be divided into lesion detection and change detection techniques. The former are those that rely on a lesion segmentation process done individually at each patient scan, needing a posterior quantification step to compute the volumetric changes. The latter involve a direct comparison of temporal MRI patient scans and are classified between intensity based approaches (image subtraction or temporal analysis techniques) and deformation based approaches, which use the obtained deformation field from a non-rigid registration process to perform the lesion analysis. Results: Observing the reported results, we have noticed the following trends. Using additional information in the segmentation process (i.e. atlases or supervised methods) produces better results but increases the user interaction and the computational time. The work of Duan et al. is an example of this pipeline which provides remarkable results with low values of the coefficient of variation measure suggesting a good agreement between experts and the automatic segmentation. Determining lesion evolution based on change detection techniques is extremely dependent on the registration accuracy. We observed that intensity based methods produce more accurate results but introduce more complexity in the process. Moraal et al. presented recently high inter-observer agreements on the lesion count by using a 3D subtraction approach. Finally, the mass effect detection of the lesions and the distinction between the tissue deformation and the tissue transformation is only possible by applying a deformation based approach. However, these methods are hard to develop and require a huge amount of computational time. Conclusion: There is not a single approach that can emerge as a standard for the clinical practice, providing an automated and accurate MS lesion evolution quantification. Future trends will focus on combining strategies to improve the available tools for the diagnosis and follow-up of MS patients.

Title (TI): Automated assessment of new lesions on subtracted MR images.
Comments: Stratifying FLAIR-hyperintense white matter lesions in a pilot study of MS, CIS, dementia and concussion using magnetic susceptibility mapping.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S157-S158), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: The number of active lesions as detected by longinterval T2-weighted subtraction (T2w-Sub) magnetic resonance (MR) imaging has been proposed (Lee M et al, Brain 1998) and recently reutilized (Moraal B et al, Ann Neurol. 2010) to provide a sensitive outcome measure for multiple sclerosis (MS) clinical trials as a complement to measures of the cumulative number of monthly gadolinium-enhancing T1-weighted (GD-T1w) lesions. The poor lesion-specific signal on subtracted images limits accurate segmentation and makes this measure operatordependent and time-consuming Objective: To develop an automated algorithm for an accurate detection of new lesions on subtracted MR images. Methods: Pilot data of a clinical trial was used for the initial evaluation of the method. The analysis was run on 19x2 pairs of T2w-Sub images (for each subject the t2-t1 and t3-t1 T2w-Sub images were created from spatially normalized datasets, where t1 was the baseline scan and t2 and t3 were yearly follow-up scans). A fixed threshold defined a priori, number of adjacent supra-threshold voxels and shape-constraints were used as joint criteria (cluster criteria) for definition of a T2w-Sub lesion. Using the contrast threshold and a cluster algorithm, voxels then were probabilistically classified as lesions. Results: Evaluation relative to manual segmentation showed only minor differences relative to the automated method with 98% sensitivity and 83% specificity in t2-t1, and 98% sensitivity and 87% specificity in t3-t1. The ratio between the new T2w- Sub lesions in 1 year and in the subsequent 2 years was about 0.5, both when lesions were counted manually (78/157) and automatically (94/181). The mean number of new T2w-Sub lesions appearing in 1 year was 4.1 by counting manually and 4.9 by counting automatically. Finally the regression between the cumulative number of monthly GD-T1w lesions and the number of automatically-detected T2w-Sub lesions was 0.93 (p<0.05). Discussion: Subtracted images seem to provide a sensitive approach to quantitative assessment of new or enlarging MS lesions. The new software that we have developed allows this to be achieved automatically, with no or minor operator intervention. Further analyses is ongoing with a larger dataset to better assess whether long-interval, automatically-defined T2w-Sub lesions provide similar sensitivity to monthly GD-T1w lesions for detection of disease activity and treatment responses in MS clinical studies.

Title (TI): Automated MS lesion segmentation and longitudinal tracking via subtraction imaging.
Comments: Longitudinal study of MS lesion evolution using neurite orientation dispersion and density imaging, relaxometry and magnetization transfer imaging.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S154), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Lesion activity on MRI is a sensitive tool in monitoring biological processes in MS. Studies have shown that subtraction imaging improves conspicuity and detection of new lesions in the context of treatment trials, and that individual lesions exhibit changes in volume and signal intensity that are not captured in aggregate findings such as lesion load and count. Objective: To develop an automated procedure to detect, delineate, and track new and changing MS lesions via subtraction MRI in order to study their spatiotemporal dynamics in largescale longitudinal datasets. Materials and methods: From an existing longitudinal dataset of individuals with MS, 4 individuals (1M, 3F) were chosen at random from patients with at least 5 scans. T1-, T2-, proton density (PD), and T2-FLAIR-weighted scans were analyzed together from each time point. All image processing was performed using Java Image Science Toolkit (JIST) with Medical Image Processing and Visualization (MIPAV). For each individual, volumes were coregistered through a longitudinal registration scheme. Each scan was normalized relative to its normal appearing white matter (NAWM) intensity. Images were compared in chronological pair-wise fashion by subtracting the most recent previous scan. Hyperintense voxels were classified into potential lesions versus noise and artifacts by characterizing the intensity histograms of the subtraction images. Lesion masks were combined across time points using a joining algorithm to maintain consistent lesion ID values, including in cases where newly appearing lesions overlapped previously existing lesions. Results: A total of 63 time points across all patients were analyzed over an average of 4.3 years per individual. On average, 19 lesions were segmented per year of study. Visual comparison showed consistent agreement between segmented lesion masks and their corresponding normalized images. ID values were preserved, allowing longitudinal analysis of image-derived measures within individual lesions. Conclusions: We propose a robust automated method for segmenting and tracking MS lesions. This enables the study of spatiotemporal dynamics in individual lesions at a level of detail and on a scale that was previously unfeasible with previous techniques.

Title (TI): The amount of peripheral CSF affects brain tissue type segmentation in MS.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S148), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: Many MR imaging analysis methods use brain/ non-brain separation. A popular software tool is FSL-BET. It aims to find the intracranial cavity (ICC) contour, but usually the outer surface is in the peripheral cerebrospinal fluid (pCSF). In diseases like multiple sclerosis (MS), the amount of lesions influences segmentation1. We hypothesized that segmentation is also affected when the ICC is incorrectly outlined, and the edge of the BET-mask is closer to the cortex. Aim: to assess whether segmentation of grey matter (GM) and white matter (WM) in MS depends on the amount of pCSF within the mask. Methods: We included 1.5T 3DT1 MR images (1x1x1mm) of 10 MS patients. Both the ICC (gold standard) and the brain surface were manually outlined. The brain mask was sequentially dilated (3x3x3 kernel) until reaching the ICC mask. The 3DT1 image was masked with each intermediate mask, and GM/WM was segmented using FSL-FAST. We calculated the GM/WM volumes for the gold standard segmentation, for the strict brain mask, and each intermediate mask. We compared volumes for each mask to the gold standard volume, and to the gold standard segmentation masked with the corresponding intermediate mask, all using paired t-tests with significance at p<0.05. Results: GM volume was reduced compared to the gold standard only for the strict brain mask (mean decrease 1.9%, p=0.009) and the first intermediate mask (p=0.048). However, WM volume was reduced for the strict brain mask and the first 7 intermediate masks, indicating that the segmentation itself is affected (mean WM volume decrease for brain mask 6.9%, p<0.001). GM volume was smaller than the corresponding masked gold standard GM volume for the brain mask, but this difference was not significant (p=0.097). By contrast, WM volumes were smaller than corresponding masked WM volumes for the brain mask and the first 7 intermediate masks (for brain mask by 6.8%, p<0.001). Discussion: This study demonstrates that the amount of CSF around the brain influences the FAST segmentation. As long as some pCSF is present, GM volume does not depend on the exact amount of pCSF, even in the presence of atrophy and MS lesions. However, WM segmentation does depend on pCSF amount. Conclusion: Strict brain edge segmentation leads to compromised FAST segmentation output in MS, especially for WM.

Title (TI): FLAIR*: A new MRI contrast for visualising perivascular distribution of multiple sclerosis lesions.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S32), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Objective: To noninvasively investigate the brain in MS with a simple and fast MRI method we call FLAIR*. The FLAIR* contrast combines T2-weighted Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery (FLAIR) and T2*-weighted contrast, which are sensitive to complementary aspects of tissue damage in MS, in a single image. Material and methods: Ten individuals with MS were scanned under an IRB-approved protocol on a Philips Achieva 3T MRI system with dual-transmit body-coil excitation and an 8-channel head coil. 3D FLAIR images were acquired using a FLAIRVISTA sequence, and whole brain 3D T2*-weighted images were collected using a flow-compensated segmented echo-planarimaging (seg-EPI) sequence. Both sequences are provided by the manufacturer on the scanner platform. Whole-brain coverage was achieved with a voxel resolution of 1x1x1mm3 for FLAIR and 0.5x0.5x1mm3 for seg-EPI. Acquisition time was 6 min for FLAIR and less than 90 sec for seg-EPI. FLAIR and seg-EPI magnitude images were rigidly co-registered and multiplied to generate the FLAIR* contrast. Results: Analysis of the FLAIR* images reveals unique contrast that is especially useful in MS, as it combines the sensitivity of both component techniques to salient pathologic features of MS. The 3D-FLAIR component defines white matter lesions and their relationship to normal brain structures, including the ventricles and cortex, in both supratentorial and infratentorial compartments. The T2*-weighted component adds information about intralesional and extralesional veins, and metal deposition. The perivenular distribution of focal lesions is clearly demonstrated in the combined FLAIR* images. Conclusions: We propose a simple and fast method for generating clinically interpretable FLAIR* images at standard field MRI. The method requires addition of a T2*-weighted segmented-EPI sequence, which covers the whole brain in 90sec. Post-processing is limited to rigid co-registration and multiplication of the FLAIR and seg-EPI images. The resulting high-quality images can be routinely obtained on MS patients at 3T and powerfully reveal the perivascular distribution of MS lesions.

Title (TI): Initial investigation of the blood-brain barrier in MS lesions at 7 Tesla.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S28-S29), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background and objective: Investigation of spatiotemporal patterns of blood-brain barrier (BBB) opening via dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) MRI gives insight into the pathogenesis of tissue damage in MS lesions. As recently shown, newly forming white matter lesions have one of two dynamical patterns of spatiotemporal enhancement: Medium-sized, early lesions enhance centrifugally (central enhancement expands into a nodule), whereas older, larger lesions enhance centripetally (thin peripheral enhancement gradually fills the lesion center). In previous work at standard field strength, characterization of these dynamics in smaller lesions (<5mm diameter) was limited, as was a detailed investigation of enhancement patterns within gray matter. Our objective was to examine spatiotemporal enhancement using high-resolution MRI at ultra-high field strength. Methods: Patients were scanned in a 7T Siemens scanner using a NOVA head coil with volume transmit and 32 receive channels. Whole-brain 3D-MPRAGE (TR=2200ms; TE=2.93ms; TI=1050ms; FA=7deg; 0.8mm3 isotropic voxels) was acquired before and after gadolinium-DTPA administration of 0.1mmol/ kg over 1min. Dedicated 3D DCE-MRI (3D-FLASH, voxel size=0.5x0.5x0.8mm; TR=9ms; TE=5.66ms; FA=13deg; 32 axial slices; AT= 32s) was acquired during and after contrast administration over 30 min. Results: We scanned 3 patients with relapsing-remitting MS and 3 patients with primary-progressive MS. DCE-MRI was performed in 5 of 11 enhancing lesions. Two lesions <3mm in diameter initially enhanced as punctate nodules and subsequently enhanced centrifugally. One lesion, 4.5mm in diameter, enhanced centripetally, evolving from a thin peripheral ring into a nodular lesion but also showing slight outward spread of the enhancing margin. Interestingly, a leukocortical lesion, 4.1mm in diameter, initially enhanced as a juxtacortical open-ring, but after 6min slight enhancement of the deeper layers of cortex was observed. Discussion: Our initial investigation of enhancing MS lesions at high resolution confirms that small lesions tend to enhance centrifugally and reveals two new features: delayed spread of enhancement into the cortex in a juxtacortical lesion and slight outward spread of enhancement from the initially enhancing peripheral ring. The timing of these events suggests that flow of the contrast agent through tissue, rather than local BBB opening, may be responsible for these patterns of spread.

Title (TI): Developmental venous anomaly as differential diagnosis of acute multiple sclerosis plaques: Findings in a cohort of 3'643 patients.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S27-S28), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Introduction: With the increasing importance of contrastenhancing (co) plaques for diagnosing multiple sclerosis (MS) in the recently revised Mc-Donald criteria it is crucial to avoid a misinterpretation of other co- abnormalities as MS-lesions. Developmental venous anomaly (DVA) is one of these pitfalls. DVAs (the most common cerebral vascular malformation (VM)) are frequent incidental findings. Due to their manifold appearance DVAs may be difficult to differentiate from acute MS lesions on a single scan but are characterized by persistent enhancement on follow up scans. The purpose of this study is to analyse the incidence and appearance of DVA on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in a large cohort of MS-patients. Material and methods: MRI-scans of 3643 MS-patients from four international multicenter-trials were studied. All patients had brain MRIs performed according to a predefined MRI-protocol including proton density/ t2w, pre- and postgadolinium enhanced t1w scans. All scans were done with 3 mm slice thickness, no gap, axial plane, in subcallosal line, on 1.5 or 3.0 T scanners. Co-t1w scans were screened by a neuroradiologist for possible DVAs that had to be confirmed on follow up scans. Possible DVAs were defined by pattern of location, anatomical area, size, shape, presence or absence of flow void or chemical shift artefacts and were counted as definite DVAs if confirmed on a follow up scan at least 6 month apart. Results: 490 (13,1%) of 3643 MS-patients had a total of 664 confirmed DVAs with an equal left/right distribution. 78 % were supratentorial , 22% infratentorial. 47% were small. Two other types of VM were identified (34 teleangiectasias, 5 cavernomas). Other imaging patterns will be described, including examples of DVAs mimicking acute MS plaques. Discussion: Our study shows that intracerebral DVAs are a very common incidental finding with at least every tenth MS-patient having such a vascular lesion. Due to different size, shape and location the typical appearance of a DVA with multiple enlarged vessels converging on a dilated parenchymal vessel is often incompletely visible. It is mandatory to recognize and to know the different MRIappearances of DVAs to avoid misjudging it as acute contrastenhancing MS lesions. Small solitary contrast-enhancing MS-plaques seen on a single MRI-scan without follow up should be checked for a clear t2-correlate, no flow void and no tubular structure before considering it for the diagnosis of MS.

Title (TI): Pathology of immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 5th Joint Triennial Congress of the European and Americas Committees for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Amsterdam Netherlands. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 17 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S17-S18), 2011. Date of Publication: October 2011.
Abstract (AB): Objectives: Natalizumab is an approved medication for highly active multiple sclerosis (MS). In rare cases, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) can be a complication of this drug. Here, we describe pathological and radiological characteristics of immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) that occurs in natalizumab-associated PML after cessation of therapy, and differentiate it from ongoing PML and severe MS rebound. Methods: Brain biopsy tissue and MRI scans from five MS patients with natalizumab-associated PML were analyzed and compared with non-MS PML. Results: Histology showed an extensive CD8-dominated T cell infiltrate and numerous macrophages within lesions, and in nondemyelinated white and grey matter, in four out of five cases. Few or no virally infected cells were found. This was indicative of IRIS as known from HIV patients with PML. Outstandingly high numbers of plasma cells were present as compared to non- MS PML and typical MS lesions. MRI was compatible with IRIS, revealing enlarging lesions with a band-like or speckled contrast enhancement, either at the lesion edge or within lesions. Only the fifth patient showed typical PML pathology with low inflammation and high numbers of virally infected cells. MRI could not differentiate between IRIS and PML. Interpretation: We describe in detail the histopathology of IRIS in natalizumab-associated PML. IRIS, ongoing PML infection, and MS exacerbation may be impossible to discern clinically alone. MRI may give some hints to distinguish different pathologies, which are definitely proven by histological characteristics. Thus biopsy seems necessary in particular cases to guide the clinicians' therapeutic decisions. Title (TI): Incorporating expert knowledge in fast semi-automated based Active Contours method in detecting multiple sclerosis lesions.
Source/Publication (SO): International Journal of Computer Assisted Radiology and Surgery. Conference: Computer Assisted Radiology and Surgery - 25th International Congress and Exhibition, CARS 2011. Berlin Germany. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 6 (SUPPL. 1) (pp S330), 2011. Date of Publication: June 2011.
Abstract (AB): Purpose: Our purpose in this study was to develop a fast globally semi-automated method for segmentation incorporating expert knowledge of White Matter (WM) and Multiple Sclerosis (MS) lesions in Regions Of Interest (ROI) within brain Magnetic Resonance (MR) images. Methods: Our purpose is to develop new globally semi-automatic segmentation based Active Contour (AC) [2] model for MS lesion incorporates expert knowledge within the formulation of AC framework. The expert knowledge integrates manual and texture information in ROI, in order to produce more reliable MS segmentation results. In particular, the user selects manually coarse RIO that encloses potential MS lesions and a sufficient background of the healthy White Matter tissues (WM) in one slice of MRI sequence. Having this two class statistic properties, we can extract texture features corresponding to health and MS lesion. Results: We applied our segmentation method to T1-weighted, T2-weighted, and FLAIR images acquired from 17 MS patients, (7 MS Male patients) and (10 Female patients). All brain MR images were acquired with a 1.5T MR system. All images were acquired with a (Figure presented) (Table presented) section thickness of 5 mm, an intersection gap of 1 mm, a field of view of 22 cm, and 16-bit gray levels. Zero-fill interpolation processing was used for reconstruction of images with 512 9 512 pixels and a pixel size of 0.4297 mm, which result in apparent high-resolution images [1]. The results obtained are compared to traditional AC with prior knowledge and an expert segmentation done by neuroradiologists (Fig. 1). In the Table below (Table. 1), we compare segmentation results for sample patients in Dice term. The Dice of the traditional AC with the prior knowledge method shows a 78,07 and our segmentation drawn a 81,52. Conclusion: Therefore, the proposed method might be feasible as segmentation tool to detect lesion MS patients in clinical practice.

Title (TI): Spinal cord injury in a patient with multiple sclerosis with secondary hydromyelia.
Source/Publication (SO): Journal of Neurology. Conference: 21st Meeting of the European Neurological Society. Lisbon Portugal. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 258 (SUPPL. 1) (pp S209-S210), 2011. Date of Publication: May 2011.
Abstract (AB): Background: MS is a demyelinating disease, characterized by multiple lesions in the white matter of the central nervous system (CNS). Cases of MS associated with spinal cord cavity diagnosed by MRI or detected through autopsy have been reported since 1985. It was assumed that the cavitary lesion was generated by a disturbance in the dynamics of the cerebrospinal fluid, caused by trauma, myelitis, arachnoiditis or spinal tumors. In MS cases it was postulated that the cavitation is caused by tissue necrosis within the lesions. However, the pathogenic mechanism of the simultaneous presence of MS lesions and hydromyelia, remains unclear up to now. Case report: A 46-year-old man, affected by bipolar syndrome since 2000, with history of drugs abuse until 2001, presented with diplopia in 1990. Silent family history. In 2002, the first brain MRI was normal. In November 2006, he had diplopia again, brain MRI and Angio-MRI showed small areas of increased signal in the cortex and subcortical regions, moderate widening of the ventricles. In September 2009, he had right lower limbs weakness and hypoesthesia, with D7 level, right ankle apallesthesia, urinary retention, EDSS was 6.0. Brain and spinal cord MRI showed multiple spinal demyelinating lesions from C5 to D9, most of them with Gd enhancement, and a central cavity from D6 to D8, suggestive of hydromyelia. Positive oligoclonal bands, VEPs were abnormal bilaterally. MS was diagnosed. In January 2010 the patient started interferon beta-1b. He had two relapses with spinal cord symptoms in March and June 2010. In August 2010 spinal cord MRI showed new dorsal lesions and worsening of hydromyelia, associated with lumbar swelling. In December 2010 the patient developed paraplegia, urinary retention and constipation EDSS was 7.0. Spinal cord MRI showed new cervical and dorsal areas, all with Gd enhancement, stable hydromyelia. The patient did not recover after steroids. Conclusions: In some cases demyelinating spinal cord lesions and hydromyelia can be associated and, as in this case, it is not easy to establish whether symptoms derive from demyelination or cord compression due to enlargement of the cavitation. It remains to be clarified whether they are two distinct diseases or the spinal cord cavitation derives from the confluence of the demyelinating lesions evolving in necrosis. It would be important to discover clinical and radiological prognostic indicators.

Title (TI): Segmentation of cortical MS lesions on MRI using automated laminar profile shape analysis.
Source/Publication (SO): Medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention : MICCAI ... International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention. 13 (Pt 3) (pp 181-188), 2010. Date of Publication: 2010.
Abstract (AB): Cortical multiple sclerosis lesions are difficult to detect in magnetic resonance images due to poor contrast with surrounding grey matter, spatial variation in healthy grey matter and partial volume effects. We propose using an observer-independent laminar profile-based parcellation method to detect cortical lesions. Following cortical surface extraction, profiles are extended from the white matter surface to the grey matter surface. The cortex is parcellated according to profile intensity and shape features using a k-means classifier. The method is applied to a high-resolution quantitative magnetic resonance data set from a fixed post mortem multiple sclerosis brain, and validated using histology.

Title (TI): Detection of Gad-enhancing lesions in multiple sclerosis using conditional random fields.
Source/Publication (SO): Medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention : MICCAI ... International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention. 13 (Pt 3) (pp 41-48), 2010. Date of Publication: 2010.
Abstract (AB): Identification of Gad-enhancing lesions is of great interest in Multiple Sclerosis (MS) disease since they are associated with disease activity. Current techniques for detecting Gad-enhancing lesions use a contrast agent (Gadolinium) which is administered intravenously to highlight Gad-enhancing lesions. However, the contrast agent not only highlights these lesions, but also causes other tissues (e.g., blood vessels) or noise in the Magnetic Resonance Image (MRI) to appear hyperintense. Discrimination of enhanced lesions from other enhanced structures is particularly challenging as these lesions are typically small and can be found in close proximity to vessels. We present a new approach to address the segmentation of Gad-enhancing MS lesions using Conditional Random Fields (CRF). CRF performs the classification by simultaneously incorporating the spatial dependencies of data and labels. The performance of the CRF classifier on 20 clinical data sets shows promising results in successfully capturing all Gad-enhancing lesions. Furthermore, the quantitative results of the CRF classifier indicate a reduction in the False Positive (FP) rate by an average factor of 5.8 when comparing to Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) and 1.6 comparing to a Markov Random Field (MRF) classifier.

Title (TI): Change detection in diffusion MRI using multivariate statistical testing on tensors.
Source/Publication (SO): Medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention : MICCAI ... International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention. 13 (Pt 2) (pp 117-124), 2010. Date of Publication: 2010.
Abstract (AB): This paper presents a longitudinal change detection framework for detecting relevant modifications in diffusion MRI, with application to Multiple Sclerosis (MS). The proposed method is based on multivariate statistical testings which were initially introduced for tensor population comparison. We use these methods in the context of longitudinal change detection by considering several strategies to build sets of tensors characterizing the variability of each voxel. These testing tools have been considered either for the comparison of tensor eigenvalues or eigenvectors, thus enabling to differentiate orientation and diffusivity changes. Results on simulated MS lesion evolutions and on real data are presented. Interestingly, experiments on an MS patient highlight the ability of the proposed approach to detect changes in non evolving lesions (according to conventional MRI) and around lesions (in the normal appearing white matter), which might open promising perspectives for the follow-up of the MS pathology.

Title (TI): Spatial decision forests for MS lesion segmentation in multi-channel MR images.
Source/Publication (SO): Medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention : MICCAI ... International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention. 13 (Pt 1) (pp 111-118), 2010. Date of Publication: 2010.
Abstract (AB): A new algorithm is presented for the automatic segmentation of Multiple Sclerosis (MS) lesions in 3D MR images. It builds on the discriminative random decision forest framework to provide a voxel-wise probabilistic classification of the volume. Our method uses multi-channel MIR intensities (T1, T2, Flair), spatial prior and long-range comparisons with 3D regions to discriminate lesions. A symmetry feature is introduced accounting for the fact that some MS lesions tend to develop in an asymmetric way. Quantitative evaluation of the data is carried out on publicly available labeled cases from the MS Lesion Segmentation Challenge 2008 dataset and demonstrates improved results over the state of the art.

Title (TI): High-resolution phased-array MRI of the human brain at 7 tesla: Initial experience in multiple sclerosis patients.
Source/Publication (SO): Journal of Neuroimaging. 20 (2) (pp 141-147), 2010. Date of Publication: April 2010.
Abstract (AB): Recent advancement for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) involves the incorporation of higher-field strengths. Although imagers with higher magnetic field strengths were developed and tested in research labs, the direct application to patient MR studies have been extremely limited. Imaging at 7 Tesla (7T) affords advantages in signal-to-noise ratio and image contrast and resolution; however, these benefits can only be realized if the correct coils exist to capture the images. The objective of this study was to develop optimized high-resolution 7T MRI techniques using high sensitivity, specialized phased-array coils, for improved gray matter (GM) and white matter differentiation, in an effort to improve visualization of multiple sclerosis (MS) lesions in vivo. Twenty-three subjects were enrolled in this preliminary study, 17 with clinically definite MS (11 females, 6 males; mean age 43.4 years; range 22-64 years) and 6 healthy controls (2 females, 4 males; mean age 39.0 years; range 27-67 years). MR imaging of MS patients at 7T was demonstrated to be safe, well tolerated, and provided high-resolution anatomical images allowing visualization of structural abnormalities localized near or within the cortical layers. Clear involvement of the GM was observed with improved morphological detail in comparison to imaging at lower-field strength. Ė 2009 by the American Society of Neuroimaging.

Authors (AU): Bo L.
Title (TI): The histopathology of grey matter demyelination in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Acta Neurologica Scandinavica. 120 (SUPPL. 189) (pp 51-57), 2009. Date of Publication: August 2009.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is characterized by focal demyelinating lesions in white matter (WM) and grey matter (GM) of the central nervous system. Results of studies using quantitative magnetic resonance imaging techniques indicate that GM MS pathology has a significant impact on clinical symptoms and disability in MS, and may occur partly independently of WM pathology. Subpial cerebral and cerebellar cortexes are predilection sites for demyelination in MS. Significant neuronal and axonal pathology has been detected in GM MS lesions. The pathology of MS lesions is location-dependent; there is less pronounced inflammation in GM lesion areas than in WM lesions. The presence and extent of GM lesions has recently been correlated to meningeal inflammation, and cells infected with the Epstein-Barr virus have been detected in meningeal infiltrates, suggesting a role of meningeal inflammation in cortical lesion pathogenesis. Ė 2009 John Wiley & Sons A/S.

Title (TI): Automatic segmentation and classification of multiple sclerosis in multichannel MRI.
Comments: Cause and prevention of demyelination in a model multiple sclerosis lesion.
Source/Publication (SO): IEEE transactions on bio-medical engineering. 56 (10) (pp 2461-2469), 2009. Date of Publication: Oct 2009.
Abstract (AB): We introduce a multiscale approach that combines segmentation with classification to detect abnormal brain structures in medical imagery, and demonstrate its utility in automatically detecting multiple sclerosis (MS) lesions in 3-D multichannel magnetic resonance (MR) images. Our method uses segmentation to obtain a hierarchical decomposition of a multichannel, anisotropic MR scans. It then produces a rich set of features describing the segments in terms of intensity, shape, location, neighborhood relations, and anatomical context. These features are then fed into a decision forest classifier, trained with data labeled by experts, enabling the detection of lesions at all scales. Unlike common approaches that use voxel-by-voxel analysis, our system can utilize regional properties that are often important for characterizing abnormal brain structures. We provide experiments on two types of real MR images: a multichannel proton-density-, T2-, and T1-weighted dataset of 25 MS patients and a single-channel fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) dataset of 16 MS patients. Comparing our results with lesion delineation by a human expert and with previously extensively validated results shows the promise of the approach.

Title (TI): Multiple sclerosis lesion segmentation using an automatic multimodal graph cuts.
Source/Publication (SO): Medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention : MICCAI ... International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention. 12 (Pt 2) (pp 584-591), 2009. Date of Publication: 2009.
Abstract (AB): Graph Cuts have been shown as a powerful interactive segmentation technique in several medical domains. We propose to automate the Graph Cuts in order to automatically segment Multiple Sclerosis (MS) lesions in MRI. We replace the manual interaction with a robust EM-based approach in order to discriminate between MS lesions and the Normal Appearing Brain Tissues (NABT). Evaluation is performed in synthetic and real images showing good agreement between the automatic segmentation and the target segmentation. We compare our algorithm with the state of the art techniques and with several manual segmentations. An advantage of our algorithm over previously published ones is the possibility to semi-automatically improve the segmentation due to the Graph Cuts interactive feature.

Title (TI): Statistical detection of longitudinal changes between apparent diffusion coefficient images: application to multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Medical image computing and computer-assisted intervention : MICCAI ... International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention. 12 (Pt 1) (pp 959-966), 2009. Date of Publication: 2009.
Abstract (AB): The automatic analysis of longitudinal changes between Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) acquisitions is a promising tool for monitoring disease evolution. However, few works address this issue and existing methods are generally limited to the detection of changes between scalar images characterizing diffusion properties, such as Fractional Anisotropy or Mean Diffusivity, while richer information can be exploited from the whole set of Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC) images that can be derived from a DTI acquisition. In this paper, we present a general framework for detecting changes between two sets of ADC images and we investigate the performance of four statistical tests. Results are presented on both simulated and real data in the context of the follow-up of multiple sclerosis lesion evolution.

Title (TI): MRI Texture Analysis in Multiple Sclerosis: Toward a Clinical Analysis Protocol.
Source/Publication (SO): Academic Radiology. 17 (6) (pp 696-707), 2010. Date of Publication: June 2010.
Abstract (AB): Rationale and Objectives: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-based texture analysis has been shown to be effective in classifying multiple sclerosis lesions. Regarding the clinical use of texture analysis in multiple sclerosis, our intention was to show which parts of the analysis are sensitive to slight changes in textural data acquisition and which steps tolerate interference. Materials and Methods: The MRI datasets of 38 multiple sclerosis patients were used in this study. Three imaging sequences were compared in quantitative analyses, including a comparison of anatomical levels of interest, variance between sequential slices and two methods of region of interest drawing. We focused on the classification of white matter and multiple sclerosis lesions in determining the discriminatory power of textural parameters. Analyses were run with MaZda software for texture analysis, and statistical tests were performed for raw parameters. Results: MRI texture analysis based on statistical, autoregressive-model and wavelet-derived texture parameters provided an excellent distinction between the image regions corresponding to multiple sclerosis plaques and white matter or normal-appearing white matter with high accuracy (nonlinear discriminant analysis 96%-100%). There were no significant differences in the classification results between imaging sequences or between anatomical levels. Standardized regions of interest were tolerant of changes within an anatomical level when intra-tissue variance was tested. Conclusion: The MRI texture analysis protocol with fixed imaging sequence and anatomical levels of interest shows promise as a robust quantitative clinical means for evaluating multiple sclerosis lesions. Ė 2010 AUR.

Authors (AU):
Filippi M.

Rocca M.A.
Title (TI): Novel MRI approaches to assess patients with multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Current Opinion in Neurology. 23 (3) (pp 212-217), 2010. Date of Publication: June 2010.
Abstract (AB): PURPOSE OF REVIEW: This review summarizes novel MRI approaches for the investigation of lesion burden and understanding of the pathophysiology of multiple sclerosis (MS). RECENT FINDINGS: Recent technical advances are improving our ability to detect and define the nature of focal lesions and 'diffuse' tissue damage in MS as well as the functional consequences of such structural abnormalities. New contrast agents allow to monitor the pluriformity of MS inflammation. Double inversion recovery sequences enable us to detect and monitor the evolution of MS lesions in the cortex. High and ultra-high field scanners are improving imaging of MS-related abnormalities at an unprecedented resolution. Furthermore, this new generation of scanners has the potential to ameliorate structural and functional MR studies of the disease. All of this has contributed, and is likely to continue to contribute, to the definition of the factors associated with the development of irreversible disability in MS. Finally, new analysis methods have allowed to track regional disease-related changes and are resulting in an increased correlation between MRI and clinical deficits. SUMMARY: Novel MR approaches highlighted previously unrecognized or neglected aspects of MS pathophysiology, which are likely to improve our understanding of the heterogeneous clinical manifestations of this condition. Ė 2010 Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.

Title (TI): A topology-preserving approach to the segmentation of brain images with multiple sclerosis lesions.
Comments: Accuracy of the compressed sensing accelerated 3D-FLAIR sequence for the detection of MS plaques at 3T.
Source/Publication (SO): NeuroImage. 49 (2) (pp 1524-1535), 2010. Date of Publication: 15 Jan 2010.
Abstract (AB): We describe a new fully automatic method for the segmentation of brain images that contain multiple sclerosis white matter lesions. Multichannel magnetic resonance images are used to delineate multiple sclerosis lesions while segmenting the brain into its major structures. The method is an atlas-based segmentation technique employing a topological atlas as well as a statistical atlas. An advantage of this approach is that all segmented structures are topologically constrained, thereby allowing subsequent processing such as cortical unfolding or diffeomorphic shape analysis techniques. Evaluation with both simulated and real data sets demonstrates that the method has an accuracy competitive with state-of-the-art MS lesion segmentation methods, while simultaneously segmenting the whole brain. Ė 2009 Elsevier Inc.

Title (TI): Brain single photon emission computed tomography with Tc-99m MIBI or Tc-99m ECD in comparison to MRI in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Clinical Nuclear Medicine. 35 (9) (pp 682-686), 2010. Date of Publication: September 2010.
Abstract (AB): Purpose: To evaluate whether or not brain single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) with Tc-99m MIBI or Tc-99m ECD (ethyl cysteinate dimer) can detect any abnormality in patients with definite multiple sclerosis (MS). We then compared these values with the results of T1, T2, and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Materials and Methods: A total of 16 patients with proved MS were enrolled in the study, and the MRI with and without gadolinium contrast and also brain SPECT with Tc-99m MIBI (8 cases) or Tc-99m ECD (8 other cases) were performed. Results: MRI studies was performed in 16 patients (13 women and 3 men, aged 16-38 years) and an average of 10.47, 3.7, 5.3, 1.7, and 0.9 lesions was found in respect in periventricular white matter, juxtacortical white matter, corpus callosum, cerebellar peduncles, and brainstem, whereas brain SPECT with Tc-99m MIBI or Tc-99m ECD detected no abnormality. In addition, 6 cases had some degree of contrast enhancement. Conclusions: It seems that brain SPECT with Tc-99m MIBI or Tc-99m ECD would not improve this insufficiency. The small sizes of some plaques, particularly in chronic atrophic form of lesions, and the possibility of deeper anatomic positions of plaques can explain to some extent why the MS lesions were impossible to delineate on brain scan, although additional studies are needed. Copyright Ė 2010 by Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.

Title (TI): Proton MRS of large multiple sclerosis lesions reveals subtle changes in metabolite T<inf>1</inf> and area.
Source/Publication (SO): NMR in Biomedicine. 23 (9) (pp 1033-1037), 2010. Date of Publication: November 2010.
Abstract (AB): The T<inf>1</inf> values of metabolites were measured in eight subjects with clinically definite multiple sclerosis (MS) having at least one large brain lesion (2.6+/-0.7mL) and in eight age- and sex-matched healthy controls. MRS examinations were conducted at 1.5T using point-resolved spectroscopy (PRESS) (TE=30ms, TR=530, 750, 1200, 1500, 3500, 5000ms). Spectra were acquired from a voxel placed in the largest lesion in the subject with MS, and in a corresponding voxel (same size and region) in normal white matter (NWM) in the matched control, and were fitted using LCModel. As there are regional variations in metabolite and water T<inf>1</inf> and metabolite signal areas, careful placement of the control voxel was necessary to measure subtle differences between the lesions and NWM. The T<inf>1</inf> and T<inf>1</inf>-corrected signal areas of creatine were the same in MS lesions as in controls. The T<inf>1</inf> values of choline were significantly shorter in MS lesions located in occipital and parietal, but not in frontal, white matter. N-Acetylaspartate (NAA) and myoinositol T<inf>1</inf> values in MS lesions were similar to those in NWM; however, the area of myoinositol correlated directly with lesion water T<inf>1</inf>, and the area of NAA correlated inversely with lesion water T<inf>1</inf>. MR spectra acquired at short TR require T<inf>1</inf> correction of choline for accurate quantification. Careful voxel placement in controls to match lesion location in subjects with MS enables a clearer view of the subtle changes in lesions. In parietal and occipital white matter, the T<inf>1</inf> of choline was shorter in MS lesions compared to normal white matter in corresponding locations in controls. T<inf>1</inf> corrected signal areas for NAA and mI were linearly related to the T<inf>1</inf> of the water in the voxel, suggesting that as the water content of MS lesions increases, the concentration of NAA decreases and that of mI increases. Authors (AU): Lucchinetti C.F.
Title (TI): The pathology of aquaporin-4 autoimmunity.
Source/Publication (SO): Journal of Neuroimmunology. Conference: 10th Course of the European School of Neuroimmunology, ISNI 2010. Barcelona Spain. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 228 (1-2) (pp 216-217), 2010. Date of Publication: 15 Nov 2010.
Abstract (AB): Neuromyelitis optica (NMO) is a CNS autoimmune demyelinating disorder that preferentially affects optic nerve and spinal cord. Clinical, pathological and serologic features, including the presence of NMOIgG, targeting the dominant CNS water channel, aquaporin-4 (AQP4), distinguish NMO from MS. AQP4 is concentrated in the plasma membrane of the astrocytic endfeet that abut capillaries and pia in the brain and spinal cord, particularly in the subpial and subependymal zones, as well as in the glial lamellae of the supraoptic nucleus in the hypothalamus. These are sites of distinctive brain MRI abnormalities observed in a subset of NMO patients. NMO pathology demonstrates variable demyelination and axonal injury, eosinophil/neutrophil infiltration, as well as a unique vasculocentric pattern of immune complex deposition, localizing to astrocytic endfeet in the perivascular glia limitans, reflecting the regional density of AQP4 molecules. In contrast to a stage-dependent loss of AQP4 in MS lesions, NMO lesions demonstrate AQP4 loss, regardless of the stage of demyelinating activity. AQP4 loss is also observed at sites of vasculocentric Ig deposition and complement activation inNMOlesions that largely lack demyelination, but are inflammatory. Such lesions have a predilection for periventricular regions, especially the medullary floor of the fourth ventricle and adjacent area postrema, a region lacking a blood-brain barrier (BBB) and rich in osmoreceptors. These lesions likely represent the pathological substrate for the intractable, but reversible, nausea and vomiting reported in some NMO patients. Supratentorial MRI brain lesions have also been described in NMO, which are either nonspecific or MS-like in appearance. These supratentorial NMO lesions pathologically resemble NMO opticospinal lesions with respect to the type of inflammation, the presence of perivascular immune complex deposits and the loss of AQP4, in a pattern distinct from that observed inMSand ADEM, and do not support an overlap of NMO with either MS or ADEM, as has been suggested by some studies. Paradoxically, cortical demyelination is absent in NMO, despite the abundant expression of AQP4 in this region. Regional differences in AQP4 concentration could contribute to this paradox, as well as regional differences in the spatial distribution or molecular orientation of AQP4 epitopes on astrocytic endfeet that might preclude efficient complement activation or intermolecular cross-linking at some sites. In keeping with in vitro studies demonstrating that exposure to NMO patient serum and active complement compromises the membrane integrity of CNS-derived astrocytes, NMO lesions are characterized by loss of astrocytes. NMO lesions also demonstrate a marked reduction in EAAT2 colocalizing to regions of AQP-4 loss. These pathological findings, coupled with the observation that AQP4 is endocytosed following binding of NMO-IgG, with concomitant loss of Na-dependent glutamate transport and loss of the excitatory transporter, suggest EAAT2 and AQP4 exist in astrocytic membranes as a macromolecular complex. Binding of NMO-IgG to astrocytic AQP4 could therefore be expected to disrupt glutamate homeostasis and lead to injury to oligodendrocytes that express calcium-permeable glutamate receptors. In summary, clinical and pathologic observations support a pathogenic role for an autoantibody of AQP4 specificity in NMO: 1) patients with acute NMO respond favorably to antibody-depleting therapies; 2) brain MRI lesions surrounding the ventricles which are typical of NMO, localize to sites of high AQP4 expression; 3) CNS lesions in NMO exhibit deposition of immunogloblulins and products of complement activation in a vasculocentric pattern that coincides with AQP4's normal distribution; 4) CNS lesions in NMO exhibit loss of AQP4 immunoreactivity, whereas active MS lesions demonstrate upregulation of AQP4; 5) Serum IgG from patients with NMO binds to the extracellular domain of AQP4; it is predominantly IgG, and it initiates two potentially competing outcomes, AQP4 endocytosis/degradation, and complement activation; 6) Paranodal astrocytic endfeet highly express AQP4; 7) Binding of NMO-IgG to astrocytic AQP-4 leads to downregulation of both AQP4 and EAAT2, with disruption of glutamate homeostasis, and 8) recent experimental animal models recapitulate some aspects of NMO pathology. These data strongly support a pathogenic role for a complement activating AQP4-specific autoantibody as the initiator of the NMO lesion, and further distinguish NMO from MS. If this IgG were to contact astrocytic endfeet, this could impact astroglial polarity and would be expected to compromise endfoot membrane integrity, osmotic regulation, BBB function and maintenance of paranodal and synaptic microenvironments resulting in irreversible tissue damage.

Title (TI): Brain single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) with 99mTc-MIBI or 99mTc-ECD in comparison to MRI in Multiple Sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging. Conference: 23rd Annual Congress of the European Association of Nuclear Medicine, EANM 2010. Vienna Austria. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 37 (SUPPL. 2) (pp S393), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Aim: To evaluate whether or not brain single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) with 99mTc-MIBI or 99mTc-ECD (ethyl cysteinate dimer) can detect any abnormality in patients with definite MS. We then compared these values with the results of T1, T2 and FLAIR in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Materials & Methods: Sixteen patients with proved MS were enrolled in the study and the MRI with and without gadolinium contrast and also brain SPECT with 99mTc-MIBI (8 cases) or 99mTc-ECD (8 other cases) were performed. Results: MRI studies was performed in 16 patients (13 women and 3 men, aged 16-38 years) and an average of 10.47, 3.7, 5.3, 1.7, and 0.9 lesions was found in respect in periventricular WM, juxtacortical WM, corpus callosum, cerebellar peduncles and bainstem, whereas brain SPECT with neither 99mTc- MIBI nor 99mTc-ECD detected no abnormality. In addition, six cases had some degree of contrast enhancement. Conclusion: It seems that brain SPECT with 99mTc-MIBI or 99mTc-ECD would not improve this insufficiency. The small sizes of some plaques, particularly in chronic atrophic form of lesions, and the possibility of deeper anatomical positions of plaques can to some extent explain why the MS lesions were impossible to delineate on brain scan. However, positron emission tomography (PET) can be complementary to other diagnostic techniques in the evaluation of MS, although additional studies are needed.

Title (TI): Tumefactive demyelination (TD) presenting with bilateral loss of vision.
Source/Publication (SO): Annals of Indian Academy of Neurology. Conference: 18th Annual Conference of Indian Academy of Neurology, IANCON 2010. Tiruchirappalli India. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 13 (5 SUPPL. 1) (pp S21), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Introduction: Multiple sclerosis lesions larger in size, having edema, mass effect and ring enhancement are unusual and also labelled as tumefactive demyelination. Presenting symptoms are tumor like and are characterised by focal motor deficit. Materials and Methods: We present a patient with TD who presented with sequential loss of vision in both eyes. Result/Case Report: A 14 year girl suddenly developed sudden onset loss of vision of right eye. There was no pain, redness or diplopia. However she was also having loss of color vision predominantly for red. She was having visual acuity of 6/60 in right eye, pupil was mid dilated and there was afferent papillary defect. Left eye was normal. MRI brain and spine was done. Brain MRI was showing multiple areas were of T2W hyperintensities with one lesion showing contrast enhancement. Spine MRI was normal. With a provisional diagnosis of optic neuritis she was given injection methyl prednisolone for 5 days with dramatic recovery of her symptoms. After 6 weeks she again had loss of vision but in both eyes and right eye being worse. MRI brain was again repeated and this time there was multiple areas of hyperintensities with one lesion having ring form enhancement, large in size and having mass effect. She was again given injection methyl prednisolone for 5 days with significant improvement. Conclusions: This is the first report of TD presenting only with loss of vision. So TD may not have tumor like presentation always as described in literature.

Title (TI): Nascent multiple sclerosis lesions are associated with early disturbances in the blood-brain barrier.
Source/Publication (SO): Clinical Immunology. Conference: 10th Annual Meeting of the Federation of Clinical Immunology Societies, FOCIS 2010. Boston, MA United States. Sponsor: Federation of Clinical Immunology Societies . Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 135 (SUPPL. 1) (pp S102-S103), 2010. Date of Publication: 2010.
Abstract (AB): Early changes in the normal appearing white matter of MS patients precede the appearance of gadolinium-enhancing lesions on MRI scans. Although these MRI studies suggest the breakdown of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) as an important feature in MS pathogenesis, limited information is available on the pathological changes occurring at the BBB during lesion genesis. Thus, this study was focused to characterize the differential changes of BBB components, particularly at the early stages of lesion formation. MS lesions were categorized as nascent, active, chronic active and chronic inactive. Sections were immunostained for distinct leukocyte markers and BBB disruption was determined by evaluating the expression pattern of fibrinogen, laminin, GFAP and the junctional proteins occludin, claudin-5, ZO1, VE-cadherin, alpha-catenin and p120-catenin. In addition, to correlate BBB disruption and endothelial activation, the expression of ICAM-1, VCAM-1 and ALCAM was studied. Nascent lesions displayed moderate leukocyte infiltration, very limited demyelination, moderate gliosis, discrete basement membrane abnormalities, considerable changes in the expression of junctional proteins and increased expression of adhesion molecules. BBB junctional proteins and basement membrane components were more severely disrupted in active lesions and the expression pattern ofmost junctional components was partially reestablished in chronic active and inactive lesions. Our pathological findings indicate the presence of early disturbances in junctional, cell adhesion and basement membrane proteins at the BBB level in non-demyelinating MS specimens. These early vascular changes coincide with perivascular leukocyte infiltration and bring pathological support for important BBB disruption in the initial stages of MS lesion formation.
