Title (TI): Serious autoimmune diseases in patient treated with glatiramer-acetate for relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis - Case report.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S286), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: Glatiramer-acetate (GA) is commonly used as first-line disease modifying drug in relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS). It is generally considered safe and well tolerated with low frequency of serious adverse events. However some autoimmune diseases like erythema nodosum or lupus-like paniculitis are rarely described in literature. We report a case of 42-year old woman with RRMS who presented with serious autoimmune disease after 10-months treatment with GA. In the history of the patient we could not identify any special risk factors. First MS symptoms occurred in May 2005, the diagnosis of RRMS was made in December 2006. The patient was treated with injections of GA since June 2007 until April 2008. At the end of GA treatment she suffered from intensive cutaneous lesions after injections and even some abscesses occurred. GA was stopped and detailed dermatological exam including cutaneous biopsy revealed suspicion to systemic lupus erythematosus, but due to negativity of antibodies this diagnosis was left. Dermatologists finally made the diagnosis of erythema nodosum. Furthermore intermittent fever complicated the situation and blood samples revealed severe pancytopenia. Subsequently the patient had several episodes of epistaxis and cutaneous haemorrhage and had to be treated with blood supplies. Blood samples showed strong positivity of anti-platelet antibodies and slight positivity of ANCA, ENA and anti-Ro antibodies. Systemic lupus erythematosus was suspected, but finally diagnostic criteria were not met. The final diagnosis was: severe autoimmune pancytopenia with haemorrhagic complications - possibly drug-induced or paraneoplastic? Treatment with cyclophosphamide intravenously and oral steroids was initiated with stabilization of haemathological parameters and also haemorrhagic complications. The course of MS switched into secondary progressive and last EDSS score of the patient is 6,0. MS lesions on brain MRI are stable and no other non-MS lesions were observed on repeated scans. Cyclophosphamide and oral steroids continue and further development of patient's health status is awaited. Conclusions: We consider pancytopenia with haemorrhagic complications to be of autoimmune origin as well as erythema nodosum. As both conditions occurred subsequently to GA treatment the relationship of these serious adverse events is possible. Furthermore systemic lupus erythematosus or lupus-like paniculitis is still not excluded.

Title (TI): Quantifying remyelination using high-field (9.4T) MRI.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S269-S270), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: Multiple sclerosis (MS) lesions in the white matter (WML) consist of demyelination, gliosis and axonal damage/loss, but remyelination also takes place. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) at 1.5 or 3 Tesla (T) is a sensitive tool to depict WML. However, distinction between demyelinated (dWML) and remyelinated (rWML) is challenging. We have shown that MRI at 9.4T enables visualization of MS lesions in the grey matter. Here, we analysed the visual and quantitative MRI characteristics of dWML, rWML and healthy looking white matter (HLWM) using quantitative MRI indices at 9.4T. Methods: Fourteen samples of post mortem MS brain were scanned to acquire the following datasets (slice thickness = 1mm, FOV = 30x30 mm): (i) high resolution spin echo (SE) T2 weighted images (matrix size = 256x256, ~117Amum in plane resolution), (ii) SE T1, (iii) SE T2 and (iv) gradient-echo with-/out saturation pulse for magnetisation transfer ratio (MTR) maps (matrix size = 256x192). Tissue blocks were then processed for embedding in paraffin and sections (immuno-)stained for haematoxylin & eosin and myelin-basic protein (MBP). Regions of interest were identified on histological sections alongside the MR images and categorized into healthy looking white matter (HLWM), dWML and rWML. Student's paired t test and regression models were applied. Results: Eighteen WML (3 rWML, 15 dWML) were identified on high resolution scans and corresponding histological sections. rWML signal on high resolution scans was higher than for dWML but lower than for HLWM. T1 in WML (dWML & rWML) was longer than in HLWM (1513 [SD 551] ms vs 1130 [425] ms, p = 0.01), as was T2 (28 [SD 8] ms vs 19 [5] ms, p = 0.01). MTR was lower in WML compared to HLWM (31.7 [SD 13.8] pu vs 47.4 [16.7] pu, p = 0.01). T1 in dWML was longer than in rWML (1023 [SD 137] ms vs 748 [142] ms, p = 0.06). No further differences emerged for any of the investigated MRI indices. Associations were detected between T2 and (i) T1 (r = 0.53; p = 0.001) and (ii) MTR (r = - 0.77, p< 0.001), however not between MTR and T1. Conclusion: MRI of post mortem MS brain at 9.4T allows detailed investigation of MR indices and their histological substrates. Our preliminary study suggest a proportion of rWML can be identified at 9.4T, and their quantitative MR characteristics - apart from lack of correlation between MTR and T1 - are plausible. Future studies will include quantitative histology of the samples and analysis of a larger number of rWML.

Title (TI): Quantitative assessment of iron accumulation in multiple sclerosis lesions using R2<sup>*</sup> relaxometry.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S266), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: Deep grey matter iron accumulation has been described in multiple sclerosis (MS) and is supposed to be implicated in MS pathology. These findings were supported by the observation of reduced signal intensities on T2-weighted images and also by quantitative MR relaxometry. It has also been speculated that increased iron levels in MS lesion strongly modulate image contrast in susceptibility weighted MRI. However, less information exists on the extent of iron deposits in MS lesions and their impact on clinical findings. R2<sup>*</sup> relaxometry, a method recently being validated in a post-mortem study at 3 Tesla, enables to assess brain iron levels in a quantitative manner. Objective: To quantitatively assess iron levels within MS lesions using R2<sup>*</sup> relaxometry and to study their relation to MS disease course and clinical parameters. Methods: Lesional R2<sup>*</sup> rates were determined by 3T MRI in 116 MS patients (mean age 36.7 (SD 9.9) years, mean disease duration 7.7 (SD 7.9) years, median EDSS 2.0 (range 0.0-7.5)), including 34 CIS, 71 RRMS and 11 SPMS patients. R2<sup>*</sup> mapping was done with a 3D multi-echo gradient echo sequence. Additionally, T2 lesion load and normalized brain volumes were calculated. Lesional R2<sup>*</sup> rates were compared to white matter R2<sup>*</sup> levels of 26 healthy controls (HC) (mean age 30.3 (SD 7.1) years) and were compared to clinical and demographical data including age, age at disease onset, disease duration, disease course, EDSS and the annualized relapse rate. Results: Lesional R2<sup>*</sup> rates were significantly decreased compared to normal white matter R2<sup>*</sup> levels of HC (p <0.005). There was no statistical significant difference between lesional R2<sup>*</sup> rates among MS patients with different disease courses. When considering the entire MS cohort, lesional R2<sup>*</sup> rates showed only a weak negative correlation with disease duration and lesion load and a weak positive correlation with brain atrophy. There was no significant correlation between lesional R2<sup>*</sup> rates and age, age at disease onset, EDSS and in RRMS patients with the annualized relapse rate. Conclusion: Our R2<sup>*</sup> relaxometry study does not support speculations of increased iron levels in MS lesions. The underlying mechanism for the iron reduction within MS lesions may result from structural tissue damage and increased water content. Furthermore, lesional iron load seems to be largely independent from age, disease stage and physical disability.

Title (TI): Strategies for automated segmentation of multiple sclerosis lesions on brain magnetic resonance imaging.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S256), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: The development of fully automated segmentation methods for assessing lesion volume in multiple sclerosis (MS), which can segment large amounts of MRI data and do not suffer from intra-and inter-observer variability, has become an active research field. Aim: To analyze the different automated methods described for segmentation of MS lesions on brain MRI. Methods: We analyzed 33 different automated MS lesion segmentation approaches with the aim of pointing out their strengths and weaknesses. Results: The different methods can be divided in supervised and unsupervised strategies. The majority of the automated segmentation approaches belong to the former group, which can be subdivided between atlas-based approaches (12 methods), that require a registration between the patient and a template, and those based on training a classifier with features extracted from manual segmentations (12 methods). In the unsupervised group we distinguish between approaches based on segmenting the tissues for obtaining the lesions (5 methods), and those based on using only the lesion properties for the segmentation (4 methods). Analyzing the qualitative and quantitative comparison of the works we observed the following trends. When evaluating the methods with the synthetic data from the Brain Web, the best results with a Dice coefficient (DSC) value of 0.87 were obtained by an unsupervised approach based on performing tissue and lesion segmentation. However, when evaluating the same approach using 7 real volumes from the McConnell Brain Imaging Centre the mean DSC dropped to 0.55. When evaluating the approaches on real data, the highest mean DSC (using 23 real volumes) was 0.78. This approach was a supervised strategy based on learning classifiers from manual segmentations. In particular, this approach segments CSF and T2 lesions using a Parzen windows classifier, while segments white and gray matter using the PD and T2 images and a parametric method. Analyzing all the methods, we noticed that the best performances in terms of DSC and also sensitivity were obtained by atlas-based supervised strategies. Conclusion: Automated segmentation of different MS lesion types in MRI is a challenging task due to the heterogeneous intensity values across the different MRI images (enhancing lesions, black holes, and hyperintense lesions). Despite the recent progress made, there is not a single automated lesion segmentation approach robust enough to emerge as a standard for clinical practice.

Title (TI): Quantification of myelin loss and repair in multiple sclerosis lesions by PET using [11C]PIB: A pilot study combining PET and multimodal MRI.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S255-S256), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is characterized by white matter demyelinating lesions with partial spontaneous remyelination. In preclinical experimental studies we have shown that the thioflavine derivative 2-(4'-methylaminophenyl)-6-hydroxybenzothiazole (PIB) could provide an interesting myelin marker able to quantify myelin loss and repair. Objective: To investigate over time the occurrence, extent and kinetic of demyelination and remyelination in multiple sclerosis lesions using positron emission tomography (PET) with [11C]-PIB. To compare these results to those obtained with multimodal MRI. Material and Methods: 6 relapsing remitting MS (RRMS) patients with at least one enhanced lesion at baseline and 4 healthy volunteers (HV) underwent PET examination using [11C]-PIB on a high resolution research tomography (HRRT Siemens Medical Solution, resolution of 2.5 mm) as well as multimodal MRI (3T Siemens System TRIO 32 channels), including magnetization transfer ratio (MTR), diffusion tensor (DTI) and spectroscopy. MS lesions were delineated on 3D-MPRAGE sequences and classified according to their localization and appearance on conventional MR images (T1 signal intensity, contrast enhancement). After co-registration with PET images, [11C]PIB uptake was quantified within each lesion and compared to the normal appearing white matter (NAWM). To assess the evolution of demyelination and remyelination in each lesion, all subjects will further undergo a second PET and MRI examination 2-3 month following the first one. Results: In HV there was a major difference between white matter and grey matter uptake, which peaked at 30min post injection and remained significant until the end of examination. In RRMS patients, the level of PIB uptake varied greatly among lesions and seemed to be influenced both by their location and contrast enhancement. Enhancing lesions trended to be less demyelinated compared to non-enhancing lesions. The comparisons of PET modifications with MRI lesion metrics (MTR, DTI, MR-spectroscopy) as well as the prospective follow up of lesions are still ongoing and will be presented. Conclusions: This study indicates that [11C]-PIB may provide an interesting marker to visualize myelin and quantify myelin loss and repair in MS patients.

Title (TI): Spatial distribution of white matter lesions in relapsing-remitting and secondary progressive multiple sclerosis - A cross-sectional comparative multicentre study.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S253), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: White matter (WM) lesions are a feature in relapsing-remitting (RRMS) and secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (SPMS). The aim of this study was to compare the regional distribution of WM lesions in RRMS and SPMS by lesion probability maps (LPMs). LPMs are a common tool to characterize in vivo MS lesion distribution, providing indirect clues regarding the mechanisms of lesion development of the entire brain, while avoiding WM aprioristic biases, and have been used in a number of MS studies.[1,2] Methods: We analyzed the spatial distribution pattern of WM lesions in T1 (TE 8.1ms; TR 400ms; voxel dimension 1x1x1mm) and T2 (TE 14ms; TR 3980ms; voxel dimension 1x1x1mm) MR images of 209 RRMS and 62 SPMS patients from two clinical centres using LPMs. MR images were acquired with two scanners (centre one: Siemens Avanto 1.5 T; centre two: Siemens Vision 1.5 T). The average lesion load was (mean[standard deviation] in RRMS/SPMS): 1718[2342]/4304[6049] voxels in T1, 5645[6669]/9591[12602] in T2 images. LPMs were obtained by using the Statistical Parametric Mapping (SPM) software. Differences in WM lesion probability between RRMS and SPMS were analyzed by using permutation-based cluster analysis (FSL-VBM) (all corrected for multiple comparisons, p<0.05). Moreover, we looked for centre-dependent differences in the distribution of WM lesions. Results: WM lesions clustered mainly around the lateral ventricles. Because the lesion distribution in both centres did not show any significant differences, and because the maximum local probabilities were similar, we compared the WM lesion distribution between RRMS and SPMS in the complete data. SPMS patients showed a significantly higher probability for T1 hypointense lesions around the lateral ventricles than RRMS patients. No significant differences were found for T2 hyperintense lesions. Conclusions: A higher regional probability around the lateral ventricles for T1 lesions (black holes) but not for T2 lesions was found in SPMS compared to RRMS. The results suggest a more pronounced impact of neurodegeneration in SPMS.

Title (TI): Longitudinal followup of USPIO enhanced lesions of clinically isolated syndrome patients.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S113), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Purpose: It has been suggested that ultra-small super paramagnetic particles of iron oxide (USPIO) was a MRI marker of macrophage activity in Multiple Sclerosis (MS) and explored another physiopathological phenomenon than gadolinium-based (Gd) contrast material, which correlates with Blood Brain Barrier leakage. Previous works have shown different patterns of USPIO enhancements of MS lesions and different normal appearing white matter (NAWM) signal changes (not seen in controls) for primary-progressive and relapsing-remitting MS. A multicenter study was initiated to explore SHU555C'USPIO as a marker for clinical predictive value of patients with clinically isolated syndrome (CIS), in association and comparison with Gd MRI and quantitative MRI (qMRI). The aim of this work is to present the preliminary radiological results of this study. Material and Methods: Five French MS centers with 3T MRI scanners are involved in this prospective study on CIS patients. Five clinical and MRI exams are scheduled every three month the first year. MRI exams include conventional and qMRI before and 24 hours after USPIO injection. Day 1 MRI consists in 3D T1, 3D FLAIR, 2D proton density and T2, 2D T1 weighted (w) sequences pre-and post-Gd, and qMRI (MTR, T1, T2 and T2<sup>*</sup> maps, DTI, CSI). Day 2 2D T1w and qMRI are performed. For the 14 first patients, acquisitions were repeated three times. A trained rater manually outlined the lesions on the T2w images for the analysis of signal changes in geometrically aligned and intensity-normalized images for the determination of contrast enhancement (USPIO/Gd+), using the 2D T1w pre-contrast as a reference. Robust Multi-Channel (T1w, T2w, FLAIR) segmentation of NAWM is used for statistical analysis of diffuse characteristics. Statistical analysis was performed on pre/post-USPIO qMRI. Results: Over the 14 patients, a subset of 4 patients, showed USPIO+ lesions, following two patterns: 1) complete enhancement of the lesion, 2) ring-shaped one, 7 patients showed Gd+ lesions. These lesions were not USPIO+ at M3. Analysis of T1 relaxometry of the NAWM, pre and post USPIO, at M0 display similar results as previously reported in RRMS and PPMS. Conclusions: As in this study abnormalities of NAWM were detected in CIS Patients using USPIO contrast agent, such longitudinal followup study should allow demonstrating whether the USPIO contrast agent might be an effective predictive biomarker of CNS tissue injury in MS.

Title (TI): Differentiation of lesions using high-field phase contrast imaging in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S102), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Objective: Imaging at 7 Tesla (7T) affords advantages in signal-tonoise ratio, image contrast and resolution. The implementation of phase MRI as an alternative imaging contrast method for brain applications has become increasingly popular in the last few years. Highfield phase imaging is promising additional information on the underlying pathology and therefore expected to be valuable for defining distinct pathological processes in the brain of patients with multiple sclerosis (MS) that are crucial for prognosis and evaluation of the disease progression. At first the aim of this study is to differentiate MS lesions according to the information gained by this novel method in MS despite their homogenous T2 signal. In a later stage patients will be examined to define the course of the disease. At this stage we present an overview on different recurring lesion pattern identified by phase contrast imaging on a 7 T scanner. Methods: We recruited ten patients who were diagnosed with relapsing-remitting MS. The scanning procedures included a 3D gradient echo sequence for phase image acquisition, a hyper turbo spin echo (TSE) 2D sequence and a high resolution 3D magnetization prepared rapid acquisition gradient echo (MPRAGE) for T1-weighted images. Results: Lesion detection was performed on T2-weighted images and compared to the scans obtained with phase imaging. Phase imaging reveals a heterogeneous contrast behavior within the lesion that had a homogenous appearance on T2-weighted scans. We did not observe an increased lesion detection compared to T2 images. According to the predominant pattern of the contrast on the phase imaging scans we divided the lesions into four groups: 1) central darkening, 2) heterogeneous darkening 3) surrounded by rim and 4) undefined. We also distinguished lesions with and without vessel penetration since phase contrast revealed to be superior in detecting microvasculature compared to the other sequences. Conclusions: In this study we demonstrate the possibility of differentiation of T2-weighted hyperintense lesions with an homogenous contrast using phase contrast imaging. We speculate that the different behavior of lesions might be related to the different macromolecule levels of lesions and/or different levels of iron due to destruction of myelin and transferrin/ferritin deposition. We suggest that specific phase pattern recognition may reflect the activity of lesions and may point to distinct pathological processes.

Title (TI): Natural course of MS: 50 years of follow-up.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 26th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis, ECTRIMS, 15th Annual Conference of Rehabilitation in MS, RIMS. Gothenburg Sweden. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (10 SUPPL. 1) (pp S13), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): J-M Charcot described "formes frustes" (burnt out cases) of multiple sclerosis. The existence of truly benign multiple sclerosis several decades after onset is still not stringently shown and supported by a description of its clinical and radiological features. Specific resources are essential for the epidemiological analysis of the frequency of these patients: An unbiased incidence cohort, defined as virtually all patients with onset within a defined geographical area and during a limited defined period of time, as a basis for a longitudinal follow-up with neutral censoring and essentially no drop-outs. We here focus on the most benign patients from the severity spectrum of a longitudinally followed multiple sclerosis incidence cohort (N=307) with onset as residents of Gothenburg 1950-1964. Two-hundred and two of these patients (excluding CIS/possible and primary progressive) had an initial remitting-relapsing course and a multiple sclerosis diagnosis according to Poser. In this cohort, the probability for remaining benign (non-progressive) 45 years after onset was 18% (N=18, SE 3.0%). At the last follow-up of the patients with relapsing-remitting course (2009-2010, 45 to 57 years after onset) three further patients had developed secondary progression, and two had died. Thirteen patients remained non-progressive, nine with EDSS 0 to 2.5, while four had stationary sequelae after attacks decades ago with isolated deficits resulting in EDSS 3 or 3.5. These 13 patients had been socially wellfunctioning, although one recently developed dementia considered unrelated to multiple sclerosis. A neuropsychological examination (n=8) showed a slight difference (p<0.01) concerning verbal memory and executive function compared to an age and socially matched reference group, while results for five other domains were within the normal range. Magnetic resonance images fulfilled the Barkhof criteria in 10/11 patients, dominated by inactive-appearing lesions in the callosum and periventricular region but few subcortical lesions. Calloso-marginal lesions extending from the inferior medial aspect of the corpus callosum were present in all but 2 patients. Classical predictors from the onset bout (remission, afferent/efferent symptoms and mono/polyfocality) strongly modified the relative risks for later progression and disability in the 45-year follow-up of the patients with initial relapsing-remitting course. Prediction from a later bout during the first 5-year-period was at least as powerful as prediction from the onset bout. Stratification according to severity with these predictors was helpful when the present natural history study was used as virtual placebo for evaluation of therapeutic impact on long-term course. However, attempts to identify the benign core group with these predictors had low specificity. In conclusion, five decades after the onset of multiple sclerosis, when reaching the population life expectancy, approximately 10% of multiple sclerosis patients from an essentially unbiased cohort had minimal neurological and neuropsychiatric disability, although they had neuroradiological signs of extensive multiple sclerosis lesions. While the clinical course may suggest a "forme fruste" at that stage, transition to the progressive stage continuestooccur more than 50 years after onset.

Title (TI): Ile587Val polymorphism of the eIF2B5 gene as susceptibility factor for multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Annals of Neurology. Conference: 135th American Neurological Association, ANA Meeting. San Francisco, CA United States. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 68 (SUPPL. 14) (pp S57), 2010. Date of Publication: 2010.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an inflammatory chronic disease characterized by a demyelinating process. Although the true etiology remains elusive, a lot of evidence have provided that genetic factors contribute to disease susceptibility. Mutations in the eIF2B gene inherited as autosomal recessive trait cause the Vanishing White Matter disease (VWM). Genetic and biochemical data of MS patient and MRI data showing VWM images similar to MS lesions, encouraged the present study in which we analyzed the eIF2B5 gene to evaluate an overlapping between MS and VWM. We analyzed 225 unrelated MS patients and 207 age- and sexmatched healthy subjects derived from the Southern Italy. A common variation Ile587Val was found very frequent in the MS patients respect normal controls. Statistical analysis suggests that polymorphism Ile587Val in exon 13 of the EIF2B5 gene should be considered as susceptibility factor in the development of MS. In conclusion, our data strongly suggest a possible involvement of the eIF2B5 in the development of MS and that variations in the MS patient strongly depend on geographic and ethnic factors, thus suggesting the importance of knowledge the epigenetic contribution in the pathogenesis of MS.

Title (TI): Fast magnetization transfer imaging of the brain.
Source/Publication (SO): Neuroradiology Journal. Conference: 19th Symposium Neuroradiologicum - The World Congress of Diagnostic and Therapeutic Neuroradiology. Bologna Italy. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 23 (SUPPL. 1) (pp 466), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Magnetization transfer (MT) reflects the exchange of magnetization between protons bound to macromolecules and protons found in free liquid. Although not used in the daily clinical routine, MT has been reported to show increased sensitivity for the characterization of pathologic brain tissue (1,2). MT effects are commonly assessed by the so-called MT ratio (MTR), reflecting a rather qualitative measure as it additionally depends on sequence parameters. In contrast, quantitative MT (qMT) parameters, such as the rate of magnetization transfer (kf) and the fractional size (F) between bound and free protons can be drawn from a tissue model, in addition to relaxation times (T1, T2). It is evident that in contrast to a simple MTR analysis drawn from two MT scans, any quantitative MT analysis requires more extensive data acquisition (3), hampering its clinical applicability. Only recently, the assessment of wholebrain, 3D high-resolution quantitative MT data has been demonstrated to be feasible within 10 minutes using a fast acquisition technique based on a MT-sensitized balanced steadystate free precession (bSSFP) protocol (4-7). MT-sensitized bSSFP provides a reliable assessment of quantitative parameters even for very small brain structures, such as the hippocampus, with a low standard deviation (8). This new framework provides a promising tool for the fast acquisition of quantitative MT parameters within the clinical routine, and especially for brain pathologies including, but not limited to: Brain tumours and metastases: An ongoing study performed on patients with different kinds of brain tumours (glioblastomas (GBM), meningeomas), and metastases demonstrates that in the different pathologies, contrast enhancing (CE) tissue and surrounding edema, which appear similar in signal intensity on conventional MRI, show differences in F, kf and relaxation times (Figure 1). Despite similar MTR for the CE areas in GBM and meningeomas, kf tends to result in higher and relaxation times in significantly lower values compared to GBM, despite similar F. Multiple Sclerosis (MS): The quantification of MTR in MS lesions with 3D-bSSFP reflects the values observed with standard 2D-GRE methods (9). In addition the i) high isotropic spatial resolution, ii) whole brain coverage, iii) stronger contrast between grey and white matter, and iv) fast and robust quantification of MTR provided by the 3D-bSSFP technique appeared advantageous over the 2D-GRE approach. Preliminary results of fast quantitative MT-based bSSFP imaging in patients with MS reveal significant differences in F and/or kf between different MS lesions behaving very similar in signal intensity on conventional MRI (Figure 2). Ischemia: Sequential MT-based bSSFP imaging in patients suffering from acute and subacute stroke showed a high linear correlation between relative MTR and the age of ischemia, indicating that MT-based bSSPF might be a promising tool for giving information about ischemic damaged brain and corresponding infarct age (range: 4 hours to 10 days) (10). Due to the unique properties of bSSFP-based qMT imaging (fast imaging, high resolution, 3D whole-brain coverage, and excellent reproducibility), this new method might be a useful sequence for tissue characterization beyond conventional MR with increased pathologic specifity for CNS lesions. (Figute presented).

Title (TI): Differentiation of acute and chronic demyelinating plaques in patients with multiple sclerosis (MS): Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) study.
Source/Publication (SO): Neuroradiology Journal. Conference: 19th Symposium Neuroradiologicum - The World Congress of Diagnostic and Therapeutic Neuroradiology. Bologna Italy. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 23 (SUPPL. 1) (pp 135-136), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Proposal: We propose quantitative approach for differentiation acute and chronic demyelinating plaques in patients with MS using DTI and MR tractography methods. Materials and Methods: Two groups of patients were studied by conventional MRI and DTI on 1.5T SIGNA EXCITE (GE). The 1-st group (PG) includes 43 patients (36-55y., disease duration from 3-6y.) with relapsingremising MS, and the 2-nd group (CG) includes 20 healthy subjects (23-60y.). Axial DTI of the brain using a single shot, multi slice spin-echo echo planar diffusion tensor pulse sequence with following parameters: TR/TE=10000/83.6ms were obtained. Using conventional MR images recorded before and after contrast injection (Gadovist, Bayer-Schering Pharma) the plaques were defined. The DT images were post processed and regions of interest (ROIs) were placed in the plaques. In addition, in the PG the ROIs were placed in the plaques and in the intact tissue of the ipsilateral hemisphere, and symmetrically, in the contralateral side of the brain on the plaque's level, and on the level of the intact tissue localization in the affected hemisphere. For comparison, in the CG ROIs positions were the same, as in the PG (see Figure 1). Results: From DTI the values of average fractional anisotropy (FA), and ADC coefficients were calculated. The FA values in both acute and chronic plaques were significantly lower than the values in the intact tissue in the ipsilateral, and in the contralateral hemisphere, and in comparison with values, obtained for subjects of the CG. ADC values were significantly higher in the acute and chronic plaques compared to all regions of the intact tissue. The DTI was not able to detect differences between ADC and FA values in the acute and chronic demyelinating plaques in patients with MS (p=0.8053 and p=0.2410, respectively). MS lesions were classified as acute, subacute, or chronic on the basis of T1 and T2 relaxation characteristics, the presence of contrast enhancement after i/v administration of Gadovist, and surrounding edema. Plaques that were considered to be acute were characterized by peripheral contrast enhancement after administration of Gadovist, by central T1 hypointensity, T2 hyperintensity, and, in most cases, by a rim of T2 edema. These lesions frequently corresponded to neurologic deficits. Other plaques were categorized as either subacute, owing to the presence of edema or contrast enhancement, or chronic, on the basis of morphology and signal characteristics. Main results of plaque classification and measurements are represented in the Table 1. ADC values in WM for all subjects of the PG elevated in comparison with the ADC values in the WM of subjects of the CG. The concentric ring appearance of acute lesions we observed on DW images in many patients during evolution of relapsing-remitting MS. Increased diffusivity with anisotropic reduction in the plaque centrum may be secondary to the combined effects of increased extracellular water and loss of myelin. Blood-brain barrier disruption in acute MS, due to astrocytic damage or functional impairment of intact endothelium or both, results in an increase in extracellular water in the perivenular region, which constitutes part of the plaque center. Oligodendrocite damage also occurs early in plaque development, resulting in demyelination and alteration of axonal conduction, which is a significant contributor to neurologic dysfunction. In the acute plaque pathologic processes evolve the rim of plaque. Variability of ADC values and anisotropic scalars in the rim in the region of active demyelination represents the presence of vasogenic edema in the extracellular space superimposed on cytotoxic edema pf oligodendroglia. Chronic MS lesions are characterized pathologically by zones of myelin loss with axonal preservation, reactive astrogliosis, and absence of acute inflammation. Extracellular water is increased relative to uninvolved white matter. These histopathologic findings would seem to correlate well with intermediate ADC elevations and a tendency toward isotropy. Conclusion: Although the pathological findings in the demyelinating plaques are diverse, the comparison FA with ADC values in the acute and in the chronic plaques (black holes) provides us very useful diagnostic information for differentiation the type of MS. DTI characterizes both of MS-plaques as lesions, presenting higher ADC and lower FA values in comparison with intact brain tissue. Typical acute plaques show a concentric ring pattern on DW images. The center of an acute plaque is characterized by marked T2 hypointensity, high ADC, and marked reduction of anisotropy as compared with the rim, intact WM, and chronic plaques. (Table Presented) (Figure Presented).

Title (TI): Magnetic resonance imaging characteristics associated with aquaporin 4 antibodies in neuromyelitis optica and neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders.
Source/Publication (SO): Neuroradiology Journal. Conference: 19th Symposium Neuroradiologicum - The World Congress of Diagnostic and Therapeutic Neuroradiology. Bologna Italy. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 23 (SUPPL. 1) (pp 135), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Purpose: Neuromyelitis optica (NMO) is a severe demyelinating central nervous system disease that preferentially involves spinal cord and optic nerve. It is now recognized that NMO is an immune-mediated disorder associated with antibodies to aquaporin 4 (AQP4), a cell membrane water channel found predominantly in the central nervous system. Immunoassays for AQP4 antibodies are now available. One assay, developed in Oxford, has been shown to detect AQP4 antibodies in more than 75% of patients with NMO. Diagnostic criteria for NMO incorporate imaging and antibody status. The typical MRI appearance is of a longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesion with a brain which is normal or does not fulfill diagnostic criteria for multiple sclerosis (MS) at disease onset. This study aims to systematically describe imaging characteristics associated with AQP4 antibodies. It is hoped that this will enhance our understanding of the role of this antibody in the NMO spectrum of disorders. Materials and Methods: A retrospective comparison of neuroimaging in AQP4 antibody positive and negative groups was undertaken. All patients had a clinical presentation suspicious for an NMO spectrum disorder and an AQP4 antibody immunoassay performed. MRI brain and/or spine of 16 antibody positive and 31 antibody negative patients were available for review. Scoring was against a proforma by two consultant neuroradiologists who were blinded to antibody status and clinical details. Results: Spinal cord lesions in AQP4 antibody positive patients were more often longitudinally extensive (> 3 vertebral segments: 100% in antibody positive patients vs. 42.1% in negatives (p=0.153)); centrally located within the cord (92.3% vs. 42.1% (p=0.0079)) and T1 hypointense (100% vs. 33.3% (p=0.142)). A minority of patients had normal brain imaging, with a comparable proportion in both groups (20.0% vs. 24.1%). No antibody positive patient had brain imaging that fulfilled Mac-Donald criteria for MS. 17.2% of the antibody negative group fulfilled these criteria. Periventricular lesions around the lateral ventricles and lesions within the corpus callosum were observed less often in the antibody positive group (33.3% vs. 68.2% (p=0.0752); 0% vs. 40.9% (p=0.0132)). Periaqueductal brain stem lesions where seen more often in the antibody positive group (33.3% vs. 4.6% (p=0.042)). Conclusion: Our observation that T1 hypointensity within a spinal cord lesion is more often seen in the presence of AQP4 antibodies is of interest. This characteristic has been proposed as a potential discriminating feature of NMO. Our data confirm that longitudinally extensive central spinal cord lesions are associated with AQP4 antibodies. A minority of patients had normal brain imaging, confirming a finding that had previously been important in the revision of NMO diagnostic criteria. In none of our antibody positive patients did brain imaging fulfill diagnostic criteria for MS. Lesions around the lateral ventricles and within corpus callosum, common sites of disease in MS, were also less common in the antibody positive group. Characteristic brain lesions have been described in NMO. Our results demonstrate that one such pattern of brain involvement, namely periaqueductal disease, was more often, but not exclusively seen in AQP4 antibody positive patients.

Title (TI): Multiple sclerosis new techniques.
Source/Publication (SO): Neuroradiology Journal. Conference: 19th Symposium Neuroradiologicum - The World Congress of Diagnostic and Therapeutic Neuroradiology. Bologna Italy. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 23 (SUPPL. 1) (pp 85-88), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is by far the most common demyelinating disorder. The diagnosis of MS continues to be based primarily upon the clinical presentation of lesions that are disseminated in time and space, but a variety of laboratory and imaging tests can contribute to the diagnosis. MRI can provide supporting evidence for the diagnosis of MS by demonstrating multifocal white matter lesions, it can exclude other central nervous system lesions that might mimic the clinical findings of MS and it can substantially reduce the time required to establish a diagnosis. Any part of the CNS can be affected by MS, including the optic nerves and spinal cord. Indeed, the most frequent presenting complaints are related to the optic nerves and/or the spinal cord. In the latter group, MRI of the brain will demonstrate multifocal white matter lesions in 40-70% of individuals, even in the absence of any clinically detectable brain lesion. This fact has led to the popular practice of doing brain MRI in all patients with suspected MS, even if all the symptoms are outside the brain. The brain lesions themselves have a predilection to the periventricular white matter (particularly the peritrigonal region) and the corpus callosum, however, any part of the brain can be affected. Lesions can also be detected in the spinal cord and optic nerves, but not as reliably as in the brain. The morphology of plaques vary on MRI. The most typical are ovoid in shape with the long axis oriented perpendicular to the corpus callosum or periventricular surface. (Figure Presented) Small lesions are the rule but the lesions can be 5-6cm or larger in size, resembling tumor. Later in the disease, confluent lesions become more prevalent. Gadolinium enhancement occurs during the active inflammatory stage of the disease and usually lasts 2-3 months. This phase of the plaque is often accompanied by edema and mass effect. Treatment with high dose steroids can suppress contrast enhancement but only for as long as the individual remains on steroids. Later the mass effect resolves and the intensity of the plaque begins to look more fluid-like, notable by relative hypointensity on T1-weighted images. Plaques may occur even in the clinically asymptomatic patients. Some of these plaques have been shown to be transient in nature. This observation has led to some therapeutic research protocols using MRI to monitor the effectiveness of experimental therapy, rather than clinical assessment. Many normal adults have small hyperintensities on T2-wighted MRI. Thus MRI must be interpreted with caution so that a diagnosis of "MS" or "consistent with MS" is not erroneously assigned to a healthy individual. Novel MR Imaging Techniques: Conventional MR imaging is associated with shortcomings including a low capacity to predict clinical status, low sensitivity to diffuse white matter involvement and to gray matter lesions. Newer emerging techniques offer opportunities for improved specificity and sensitivity in diagnosing and monitoring MS, enhancing our ability to detect and characterize the disease burden, including occult microscopic disease invisible when using conventional MR techniques. Volumetric MR imaging Lesion quantification and brain atrophy: Volumetric MR imaging provides quantification of disease burden by assessing the lesion load on T2 and T1 images (black holes, total enhancing load), and is used in monitoring the natural history of the disease and in clinical trials. Quantification of diffuse atrophy reflects the adverse outcome of pathology and is seen even in the early stages of disease, including CIS. There are a number of computer-assisted manual, semi, or automated techniques for quantification which are mainly based on programs developed locally. A standardized, reliable, quick and operator friendly technique needs to be developed and tested before it can be used in the clinical setting. Magnetization transfer imaging (MTI): MTI offers greater pathologic specificity for macromolecules such as myelin and is quantified by calculating the magnetization transfer ratio (MTR), which provides an imaging marker for myelin disorder. MTR is reduced in ms lesions, in the perilesional area, in dirty appearing white matter and, finally, in white matter that appears normal on conventional MR images (NAWM). By applying an MTR histogram based on whole brain tissue, the global MTR histogram allows for the evaluation of data from all pixels of the brain and thus provides a more complete picture of disease burden, including the substantial occult pathology of NAWM. Compared to conventional MR imaging, MTI has shown increased pathologic specificity and high sensitivity to NAWM abnormalities. Thus MTI offers a noninvasive tool for imaging myeline disorder, remyelination process and occult pathology in MS. Diffusion tensor imaging: MS lesions are pathologically heterogeneous and show highly variable values of diffusion indices. In general lesions have an increased mean diffusivity (MD) or ADC and decreased fractional anisotropy (FA) compared to normal white matter, which indicates disruption of myeline and axonal structures that leads to disorganization and increases extracellular space. Again, just as seen with MTR, water diffusion abnormality is documented in the NAWM. Higher mean diffusivity and lower fractional anisotropy is seen in lesions compared to NAWM, and higher mean diffusivity and lower fractional anisotropy is seen in NAWM compared to normal white matter. As with MTI, DTI can also be assessed globally by using histogram analysis. In addition, specific fiber tracts can be visualised on tractography. Fewer fibers have been observed in the corticospinal tract at the brainstem level in patients with a higher cerebral lesion load supporting the concept of wallerian degeneration in MS. MR spectroscopy: MR spectroscopy has enabled the in vivo study of certain metabolites. In MS lesions the most remarkable finding is the reduction of NAA peak compared with other metabolites. A nonlocalized MR spectroscopy sequence has been developed for quantifying whole-brain NAA (WBNAA) concentrations. A decline in WBNAA is seen in patients with MS compared with matched controls. MR imaging of gray matter lesions: Histopathologic studies have shown that a substantial portion of the total cerebral lesion load is located within the cerebral neocortex but also in the thalamus, basal ganglia, hypothalamus, hippocampus, cerebellum, and spinal cord. With a double inversion (DIR) MR sequence, inversion times can be selected to suppress the signals from both the CSF and the white matter. The images show superior delineation of gray matter and detection of cortical lesions is improved. (Figure Presented) Appreciating gray matter pathology by imaging, might, therefore, provide new insight, improving our understanding of MS pathobiology, and providing additional markers to monitor disease evolution, and treatment response. f-MRI: Despite the sensitivity of MR derived quantitative parameters to disease changes and tissue integrity, their correlation to clinical scales of impairment and disability are still scanty. f-MRI studies in MS patients have shown that there are adaptive mechanisms of brain plasticity which might contribute to limiting clinical consequences of tissue injury. Studies have investigated the patterns of cortical activation in patients with MS during the performance of motor, visual and cognitive paradigms and have shown that brain activation patterns are altered compared to controls, indicating that there is cortical reorganization in patients affected by this condition. Failure or exhaustion of such mechanisms with increasing disease duration or burden might be among the factors contributing to irreversible clinical disability. High field MRI: With high-field strength MR imaging (3.0 T and higher), high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) can be achieved, allowing for thinner sections and higher-resolution matrices which can increase the number of detectable lesions, provide better appreciaton of lesion heterogeneity and better detection of cortical lesions. Highfield strength MRI presents a new set of challenges that require extensive refinement during the next few years. In the past 2 decades, MR imaging has brought about dramatic changes in the evaluation of MS, including diagnosis, natural history, and therapeutic monitoring. Using novel imaging techniques, several new imaging concepts have been developed through the study of MS-including "lesion load," "NAWM," "MTR," and "WBNAA". Combining different MR modalities, which are sensitive to different aspects of MS pathology, is a promising way to increase further our understanding of the mechanisms involved in the pathophysiology of this disease. Nevertheless, there are many remaining challenges in front of us. New techniques need to be refined and validated before they can be integrated properly into clinical research and practice. New acquisition schemes and analysis procedures require standardization and optimization so that they can be used in multi-site settings. According to current consensus, T2 and gadolinium-enhanced T1MRimaging remain mainstay approaches in practice. MS is a challenging disease in all aspects ranging from etiology to diagnosis and treatment. It is also a disease that has greater heterogeneity in terms of clinical forms, imaging appearance, and treatment response. With the ever-advancing technology, MR imaging will certainly further improve our understanding of this disease and continue to play an extremely important role going forward.

Title (TI): Imaging of white matter diseases.
Source/Publication (SO): Neuroradiology Journal. Conference: 19th Symposium Neuroradiologicum - The World Congress of Diagnostic and Therapeutic Neuroradiology. Bologna Italy. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 23 (SUPPL. 1) (pp 84), 2010. Date of Publication: October 2010.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is the most important white matter disease, but leukoaraiosis (age-related white matter lesion) is the most frequently encountered white matter lesion. In this talk, multiple sclerosis, leukoaraiosis, and other white matter diseases are reviewed. MS is an autoimmune, inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system. MS shows multiple lesions in both space and time. The revised McDonald criteria are the most widely used diagnostic criteria for MS in Western countries, although some modifications of the diagnostic criteria are needed in Asian countries, as Asian patients show fewer lesions and differences in lesion distributions. Wide variations exist in MS lesions, including ovoid lesions, callosal-septal interface lesions, isolated U-fiber lesions (juxtacortical lesions), tumefactive lesions and Balo's concentric lesions. MR imaging is important not only as a diagnostic tool of MS, but also as a biomarker of this disease. Neuromyelitis optica (NMO) is an idiopathic, severe, demyelinating disease of the central nervous system that preferentially affects the optic nerve and spinal cord. NMO is more frequent in Asia and was formerly thought to be a variant of MS by researchers in Asian countries. However, this disease is now considered to be a distinct entity, since a specific antibody for this disease (NMO-immunoglobulin G or aquaporin 4 antibody) has been identified. Some differences in brain and spinal cord lesions are seen between MS and NMO. Acute demyelinating encephalomyelitis (ADEM) is a severe inflammatory demyelinating disease that frequently develops secondary to infection or vaccinations. ADEM is usually monophasic, but may recur or evolve to MS. Diagnostic criteria for differentiation between ADEM and MS have been proposed. Leukoaraiosis is an age-related degeneration of deep cerebral white matter, characterized by hyperintensity on T2-weighted or FLAIR imaging. Leukoaraiosis does not always represent infarction. Several risk factors for leukoaraiosis have been reported, including aging, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, metabolic syndrome, atherosclerosis, genes and carotid plaques. A variety of pathological changes has also been reported to correspond to leukoaraiosis. Fazekas classification is widely used to visually classify leukoaraiosis, and computer-assisted quantification of leukoaraiosis has also been reported. Questions for CME 1. Which of the following criteria is currently regarded as the gold standard for multiple sclerosis diagnosis? a. Poser criteria b. Schumacher criteria c. McDonald criteria d. Wendy's criteria Answer: c. (McDonald criteria) 2. Which of the followings is the target of NMO-immunoglobulin G? a. Aquamarine-3 water channel b. Aquamarine-4 water channel c. Aquaporin-3 water channel d. Aquaporin-4 water channel Answer, d. (Auaporin-4 water channel).

Title (TI): A quantitative study of perfusion using a gadolinium contrastagent for characterization of multiple sclerosis lesions.
Comments: Functional connectivity alterations reveal complex mechanisms based on clinical and radiological status in mild relapsing remitting multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 15th Annual ACTRIMS Meeting Americas Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. San Antonio, TX United States. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 16 (8) (pp 1011), 2010. Date of Publication: August 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: While MRI is used to confrm the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis (MS), conventional MRI techniques have shown a limited ability to demonstrate pathological changes characteristic of the disease. Standard MRI in MS includes imaging after the injection of gadolinium (Gd) based contrast agents (CAs). Gd enhancement is indicative of a breakdown in the blood brain barrier (BBB) and visually enhancing lesions have been interpreted as active. Simply characterizing lesions as enhancing or non-enhancing, however, neglects information that is present in the enhancement patterns of the lesions. Objectives: In this study, we analyze the patterns of enhancement in enhancing MS lesions, T<inf>1</inf>-hypointense non-enhancing lesions, and T<inf>1</inf>-isointense non-enhancing lesions in dynamic contrast enhanced (DCE) MRI data using a two compartment model. We aim to show that additional information can be gained about MS lesions through quan-tifcation of pharmacokinetic parameters. Methods: In a preliminary analysis of 3-Tesla MRI data from 37 MS patients, we defned regions of interest (ROIs) in DCE images for 109 lesions identifed in fuid attenuation inversion recovery (FLAIR) images and matched control ROIs in normal appearing brain matter. Signal enhancement curves were evaluated using Brix's two compartment model, enabling calculation of the following pharmacokinetic parameters: exchange rate (kep), amplitude Amp, and elimination rate (kel). Results: Statistically signifcant differences were found between the calculated values of Amp (0.23 vs. 0.07, p<0.0001) and kel (0.13 vs. 0.27, p=0.026) from enhancing lesions and Amp (0.09 vs. 0.06, p=.028) from T<inf>1</inf>-hypointense lesions with respect to their matched control values. Conclusions: This study shows that Gd based contrast agents can be used in MRI to show more than just the presence of enhancing lesions in MS patients. DCE MRI can detect perfusion differences between MS lesions and normal appearing brain matter.

Title (TI): Loss of Purkinje cells in association with cerebellar plaques in the classical Charcot and the acute Marburg variants of multiple sclerosis: Two case studies.
Source/Publication (SO): Brain Pathology. Conference: 17th International Congress of Neuropathology, ICN 2010. Salzburg Austria. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 20 (SUPPL. 1) (pp 82-83), 2010. Date of Publication: September 2010.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system (CNS) involving mainly the white matter. In the past 10-15 years, a paradigm shift has occurred in MS pathology: axonal and neuronal loss have been recognized as a widespread finding in MS lesions and they are currently considered a major substrate for the permanent neurological deficits. Recently, pathology and MRI studies have shown that in addition to white matter lesions, there is significant involvement of the gray matter. Involvement of the cerebellum in MS is infrequently remarked upon. A review of standard texts of neuropathology reveals almost no mention of cerebellar involvement. Unlike cerebral gyral white matter, cerebellar folial white matter is small, with tertiary folia measuring no more than 0.2 mm in width. This remains below the resolution of present day MRI techniques. A recent report has described extensive demyelination in the cerebellar cortex associated with relative axonal and neuronal preservation. Here, we report Purkinje cell loss in two cases of multiple sclerosis, one of the classical Charcot variant and the other of the acute Marburg variant.

Title (TI): Periaqueductal lesions in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Journal of Neurology. Conference: 20th Meeting of the European Neurological Society. Berlin Germany. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 257 (SUPPL. 1) (pp S131), 2010. Date of Publication: June 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: The aqueduct of Sylvius is a small channel (1.5-1.8 cm in length, 1-2 mm in width) that traverses the midbrain connecting the third and fourth ventricle. The periaqueductal (PA) tissue consists of grey matter implicated in pain modulation. Multiple sclerosis (MS)lesions in this area are relatively frequent. We sought to investigate PA lesions further by using a large cohort of MS patients and sensitive Magnetic Resonance (MR) sequences. Methods: In 260 MS patients who participated in a genotype/ phenotype MS study the thin slice Proton Density-weighted (PD-w) axial MR images were analysed. Two experienced readers evaluated the images by consensus. Normal MRI characteristics of the PA area were determined in 11 healthy control subjects. The brainstem/midbrain area was evaluated on 5 consecutive 3mm slices. MS studies were rated as abnormal when an asymmetric hyperintense lesion was identified on a minimum of 2 consecutive slices. In 23 patients with PA lesions depicted on PD-w images further data were acquired with a 3D Double Inversion Recovery (DIR) sequence that shows the highest contrast of grey matter lesions. The DIR images were used to describe the signal presentations and topographical relationship of PA lesions to surrounding structures in further detail. Results: The conservative image review process identified periaqueductal abnormalities in 50 of 260 MS patients (19%) on PD-w images. Multiple logistic regression analysis revealed that total brain T1-hypointense (p=0.002) and T2 hyperintense (p=0.002) lesion volumes correlated with the presence of PA lesions, while gender, age, disease duration, disease course and EDSS did not correlate. Multiplanar analysis of DIR images showed that PA lesions were mainly isolated lesions involving the surrounding grey matter and white matter, in some cases they were extensions of periventricular subependymal lesions of the 3rd ventricle extending towards the aquaeduct and involving the PA grey matter. Conclusions: PA lesions involving the surrounding grey matter and white matter are common in MS and can in some cases be extensions from subependymal periventricular 3rd ventricle lesions rostrally towards the aqueduct. The association with T2w- and T1wlesion volumes indicates that this lesion location belongs to the spectrum of typical MS lesion sites. The DIR sequence is of particular value as it allows to identify such lesions easier due to its strong lesion to grey matter (and white matter) contrast.

Title (TI): Voxel based morphometry with DARTEL in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Neurotherapeutics. Conference: 12th Annual Meeting of the American Society for Experimental NeuroTherapeutics, ASENT. Bethesda, MD United States. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 7 (3) (pp 330), 2010. Date of Publication: July 2010.
Abstract (AB): Background: Voxel based morphometry (VBM) is an automated tool used to assess the topography and magnitude of cerebral atrophy from MRI scans. However, VBM output is highly dependent on the input provided by registration and segmentation algorithms. Diffeomorphic anatomical registration through exponentiated lie algebra (DARTEL) is a new tool which has recently been developed to improve registration in healthy volunteers (HV) and neurodegenerative diseases. This has not yet been applied to multiple sclerosis (MS). Objective: To assess regional gray matter (GM) and white matter (WM) atrophy using VBM combined with DARTEL in MS patients. Methods: Using a 3T MRI scanner, fluid-attenuated inversion-recovery (FLAIR) and 3D spoiled gradient recalled (SPGR) scans were acquired from 26 relapsing remitting (RR) MS patients [age (mean +/- SD) 39.2 +/- 9.4 years, disease duration 8.5 +/- 5.9 years, Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS) score 1.2 +/- 0.9, FLAIR lesion volume (FLV) 15.0 +/- 14.7 mL] and 28 sex- and age-matched HV. MS lesions on SPGR and FLAIR images were traced using a semi-automated thresholding tool. VBM was performed using DARTEL with statistical parametric mapping (SPM8) software from SPGR images. An analysis of covariance was used to assess differences in GM and WM volume between MS patients and HV. Age, gender, and intracranial volume were included as covariates. Results: Compared with HV, MS patients showed significant GM atrophy in the bilateral thalami, bilateral caudate, and right putamen. Furthermore, WM atrophy was found in the brainstem, corpus callosum, fornix, and bilateral temporal and occipital lobes. FLV significantly correlated with GM atrophy in the thalami and with WM atrophy in the brainstem, temporal lobes, and corpus callosum. Conclusions: VBM-DARTEL reveals widespread WM and GM cerebral atrophy affecting many areas of the brain, which is associated with overt lesions in MS patients. This pilot study suggests that VBM-DARTEL is a promising tool to derive meaningful disease specific information in MS.

Title (TI): Anomalous venous blood flow and iron deposition in multiple sclerosis.
Comments: MRI evidence of acute inflammation in leukocortical lesions of patients with early multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Journal of Cerebral Blood Flow and Metabolism. 29 (12) (pp 1867-1878), 2009. Date of Publication: December 2009.
Abstract (AB): Multiple sclerosis (MS) is primarily an autoimmune disorder of unknown origin. This review focuses iron overload and oxidative stress as surrounding cause that leads to immunomodulation in chronic MS. Iron overload has been demonstrated in MS lesions, as a feature common with other neurodegenerative disorders. However, the recent description of chronic cerebrospinal venous insufficiency (CCSVI) associated to MS, with significant anomalies in cerebral venous outflow hemodynamics, permit to propose a parallel with chronic venous disorders (CVDs) in the mechanism of iron deposition. Abnormal cerebral venous reflux is peculiar to MS, and was not found in a miscellaneous of patients affected by other neurodegenerative disorders characterized by iron stores, such as Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Several recently published studies support the hypothesis that MS progresses along the venous vasculature. The peculiarity of CCSVI-related cerebral venous blood flow disturbances, together with the histology of the perivenous spaces and recent findings from advanced magnetic resonance imaging techniques, support the hypothesis that iron deposits in MS are a consequence of altered cerebral venous return and chronic insufficient venous drainage. Title (TI): Imaging correlates of leukocyte accumulation and CXCR4/CXCL12 in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Archives of Neurology. 66 (1) (pp 44-53), 2009. Date of Publication: January 2009.
Abstract (AB): Objective: To compare leukocyte accumulation and expression of the chemokine receptor/ligand pair CXCR4/CXCL12 in magnetic resonance imaging-defined regions of interest (ROIs) in brains from patients with chronic multiple sclerosis. We studied the following ROIs: normal-appearing white matter (NAWM); regions abnormal only on T2-weighted images (T2 only); and regions abnormal on T2- and T1-weighted images with an abnormal magnetization transfer ratio (T2/T1/MTR). Design: Case-control study. Setting: Cleveland Clinic. Patients: Brain tissue was acquired from 5 patients with secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (MS) and 5 non-neurological controls. Intervention: Magnetic resonance imaging pathological correlations were performed on the 5 cases. Based on imaging characteristics, 30 ROIs were excised. Main Outcome Measure: Using immunohistochemical analysis, we evaluated myelin status, leukocyte accumulation, and CXCR4/CXCL12 expression in the MS ROIs and white matter regions from the 5 nonneurological controls. Results: Eight of 10 T2/T1/MTR regions were chronic active or chronic inactive demyelinated lesions, whereas only 2 of 10 T2-only regions were demyelinated and characterized as active or chronic active lesions. Equivalent numbers of CD68<sup>+</sup> leukocytes (the predominant cell type) were present in myelinated T2-only regions as compared with NAWM. Parenchymal T cells were significantly increased in T2/T1/MTR ROIs as compared with T2-only regions and NAWM. Expression of CXCR4 and phospho-CXCR4 were found on reactive microglia and macrophages in T2-only and T2/T1/MTR lesions. CXCL12 immunoreactivity was detected in astrocytes, astrocytic processes, and vascular elements in inflamed MS lesions. Conclusions: Inflammatory leukocyte accumulation was not increased in myelinated MS ROIs with abnormal T2 signal as compared with NAWM. Robust expression of CXCR4/CXCL12 on inflammatory elements in MS lesions highlights a role of this chemokine/receptor pair in central nervous system inflammation. Ė2009 American Medical Association. Title (TI): Comparison of MRI signatures in pattern I and II multiple sclerosis models.
Source/Publication (SO): NMR in Biomedicine. 22 (10) (pp 1014-1024), 2009. Date of Publication: 2009.
Abstract (AB): The majority of individuals with multiple sclerosis (MS) exhibit T-cell- and macrophage-dominated lesions (patterns I and II; as opposed to III and IV). These lesions, in turn, may be distinguished on the basis of whether or not there are immunoglobulin and complement depositions at the sites of active myelin destruction; such depositions are found exclusively in pattern II lesions. The main aim of this study was to determine whether pattern I and pattern II MS lesions exhibit distinct MRI signatures. We have used a recently described focal MOG-induced EAE model of the rat brain, which recapitulates many of the hallmarks of pattern II MS; we compared this with our previous work in a delayed type hypersensitivity model of a pattern I type lesion in the rat brain. Demyelinating lesions with extensive inflammation were generated, in which the T2-weighted signal was increased. Magnetisation transfer ratio (MTR) maps revealed loss and subsequent incomplete recovery of the structure of the corpus callosum, together with changes in tissue water diffusion and an associated increase in ventricle size. Notably, the MTR changes preceeded histological demyelination and may report on the processes leading to demyelination, rather than demyelination per se. Immunohistochemically, these MRI-detectable signal changes correlated with both inflammatory cell infiltration and later loss of myelin. Breakdown of the blood-brain barrier and an increase in the regional cerebral blood volume were also evident in and around the lesion site at the early stage of the disease. Interestingly, however, the MRI signal changes in this pattern II type MS lesion were remarkably consistent with those previously observed in a pattern I lesion. These findings suggest that the observed signal changes reflect the convergent histopathology of the two models rather than the underlying mechanisms of the disease. Title (TI): Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy and relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis: A comparative study.
Source/Publication (SO): Archives of Neurology. 66 (5) (pp 593-599), 2009. Date of Publication: May 2009.
Abstract (AB): Objective: To identify clinical and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) features that distinguish progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) from relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS). Design: Retrospective medical record review. Setting: Two urban teaching hospitals in Detroit, Michigan. Patients: Forty-five confirmed PML cases and 100 patients with RRMS. Main Outcome Measures: Clinical and MRI features distinguishing PML from RRMS. Results: Overall, monosymptomatic presentations were more common in multiple sclerosis (MS) than PML (85% vs 47%; P<.01). However, patients with PML presented more often with hemiparesis (24% vs 5%; P=.001) and altered mentation (19% vs 0%; P<.0001), whereas brainstem (2% vs 18%; P=.007) presentations were more common in patients with RRMS. Spinal cord and optic neuritis presentations were seen in 18% and 33% of patients patients with RRMS, respectively, but not in patients with PML (P<.0001). Brain MRI scans, available in 35 (78%) PML cases, revealed 7 lesion types. Large, confluent T2-weighted lesions (74% vs 2%; P<.0001) and deep gray matter lesions (31% vs 7%; P<.001) were more frequent in patients with PML than patients with RRMS. Crescentic cerebellar lesions (23% vs 0%; P<.001) were seen only in patients with PML. Gadolinium-enhancing (23%), transcallosal (9%), and periventricular (9%) lesions were noted in patients with PML. Brain magnetization transfer ratio (MTR) was low in both PML and MS lesions. However, normal-appearing brain tissue MTR in PML was higher than normal-appearing brain tissue MTR in RRMS (44.15% vs 41.04%; P=.002), suggesting that PML may be relatively more focal than MS. Conclusions: There appear to be differences between the clinical and MRI characteristics of PML and RRMS, which may help distinguish new MS activity from PML. Magnetization transfer ratio studies may provide additional clues in improving early detection of PML in patients with preexisting MS and warrant further investigation. Title (TI): Use of ultrasmall superparamagnetic particles of iron oxide (USPIO)-enhanced mri to demonstrate diffuse inflammation in the normal-appearing white matter (NAWM) of multiple sclerosis (MS) patients: an exploratory study.
Source/Publication (SO): Journal of Magnetic Resonance Imaging. 29 (4) (pp 774-779), 2009. Date of Publication: April 2009.
Abstract (AB): Purpose: To explore ultrasmall superparamagnetic particles of iron oxide (USPIO) as a marker for diffuse inflammation in multiple sclerosis (MS) normal-appearing white matter (NAWM), using quantitative MRI. Disease activity in the NAWM of MS patients partly explains why MRI lesion burden correlates only moderately with disability. USPIO have been shown to visualize the cellular component of inflammation in focal MS lesions. In this study, we aimed to explore USPIO as a marker for the more diffuse inflammation in MS NAWM, using quantitative MRI. Materials and Methods: In this prospective MRI study, 16 MS patients (eight relapsing-remitting MS [RRMS] and eight primary-progressive MS [PPMS] cases) and five healthy control (HC) subjects were included. Using a flip- angle (FA) array, B1-corrected T1 maps were generated before and 24 hours after USPIO (SHU555C) injection. White-matter (WM) T1 histogram and region-of-interest (ROI) characteristics were compared between both time points using Wilcoxon signed-rank test. Results: Both NAWM ROI and histogram analyses showed T1 shortening after USPIO injection in MS patients (P < 0.01), but not in HCs (P = 0.68). Conclusion: This exploratory study suggests that USPIO- enhanced MRI may be a new potential marker for subtle inflammatory activity in MS NAWM. Further studies should focus on relating diffuse inflammation to clinical disease activity and treatment efficacy. Ė 2009 Wiley-Liss, Inc.

Title (TI): First clinical study on ultra-high-field MR imaging in patients with multiple sclerosis: Comparison of 1.5T and 7T.
Source/Publication (SO): American Journal of Neuroradiology. 30 (4) (pp 699-702), 2009. Date of Publication: April 2009.
Abstract (AB): Background and Purpose: Higher magnetic field strengths and continuous improvement of high - resolution imaging in multiple sclerosis (MS) are expected to provide unique in-vivo and non-invasive insights in pathogenesis and clinical monitoring. The purpose of this study was to investigate the potential of high-resolution imaging of MS lesions in vivo comparing 7T with conventional 1.5T. MATERIALS AND METHODS: Twelve consecutive patients with clinically definite MS were scanned on a 7T whole-body scanner and on a 1.5T Avanto. The 1.5T and 7T imaging protocol consisted of high-resolution axial proton density (PD) + T2-weighted turbo spin-echo and T2*-weighted gradient - echo (GRE), and sagittal T1-weighted 3D magnetization-prepared rapid acquisition of gradient echo. Results: The sequence parameters at 7T had to be modified because of specific absorption rate (SAR) restrictions while keeping contrast parameters equivalent to 1.5T. White matter lesions were better detected and delineated from adjacent structures at 7T compared with 1.5T. There were 42% of the patients who showed additional lesions at 7T: there were 97 white matter lesions detected on 1.5T versus 126 lesions at 7T, an increase of 23%. The perivascular migration of MS lesions was well visualized on T2*-weighted GRE sequences. In larger lesions (10 mm), a multilayer structure was revealed on T2*-weighted GRE not seen at 1.5T. Because of the higher resolution, it was possible to differentiate between juxtacortical white matter lesions and cortical lesions. There were 44% of the subcortical lesions depicted at 7T that showed cortical involvement. Conclusions: Ultra-high-field imaging of patients with MS at 7T was well tolerated and provided better visualization of MS lesions in the gray matter and demonstrated structural abnormalities within the MS lesions themselves more effectively.

Title (TI): Magnetic resonance imaging in multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Topics in Magnetic Resonance Imaging. 20 (6) (pp 313-323), 2009. Date of Publication: December 2009.
Abstract (AB): Objectives: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an inflammatory disease of unknown origin affecting the central nervous system. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) plays an increasingly important role in its diagnosis and further monitoring of disease progress. Methods: The typical MRI appearance of MS on conventional MRI sequences and current diagnostic criteria for MS are discussed. Advanced imaging techniques are reviewed with respect to application in MS. Finally, the atypical variants of MS are briefly reviewed. Conclusions: Although MRI is not intended and will not replace clinical assessment in MS, the recognized MRI criteria may aid in establishing an earlier and more accurate diagnosis of MS in the context of a clinical suspicion or clinically isolated syndrome. In addition, MRI might contribute to rule out differential diagnoses for MS. Moreover, MRI may be used to monitor the evolution of MS and in pharmaceutical trials. Advanced imaging techniques might, in the future, further characterize MS lesion subtypes and potentially guide tailored therapy. Copyright Ė 2010 by Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.

Title (TI): Severe relapses after the first infusion of natalizumab in active relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis.
Source/Publication (SO): Multiple Sclerosis. Conference: 25th Congress of the European Committee for Treatment and Research in Multiple Sclerosis. Dusseldorf Germany. Conference Publication: (var.pagings). 15 (9 Suppl. S) (pp S234), 2009. Date of Publication: September 2009.
Abstract (AB): Background: Natalizumab, a humanized recombinant monoclonal antibody (mAb), that binds the a4p1 integrin, blocks the migration of inflammatory cells from the peripheral blood into the brain, a relevant event in the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis (MS) lesions. In Italy, natalizumab is currently used to treat relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) patients with very active disease at onset and patients not responding to the other approved immunomodulatory agents (IMA). Objective: To describe three RRMS patients with very active disease (high relapse rate and Gad-enhancing lesions on MRI) that had severe clinical exacerbation after the first infusion of natalizumab. Results: Three female patients out of 56 RRMS treated with natalizumab experienced a severe clinical relapse, associated with a marked increase in the expanded disability status scale (EDSS) score and in brain inflammation as assessed by Gad-enhancing lesions and increased T2 lesion volume at MRI. All relapses occurred 6 to 8 days after the first infusion of the mAb. All patients required a sustained anti-inflammatory treatment with methylprednisolone 1 gr iv/die for more than five days. Two patients did not recover from relapse, and complained a significant increase in EDSS, and they withdraw natalizumab. One patient fully recovered from relapse and remained on natalizumab treatment. Conclusions: Although the mechanism by which natalizumab may worsen MS is unknown, our findings are consistent with studies on experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis, in which natalizumab worsened disease evolution when administered during active phases of the disease, possibly by altering the regulatory cell network in the brain. Our observations suggest that patients having complained a recent relapse and/or with Gad-enhancing lesions on a recent brain MRI should be treated with natalizumab only after a course of high-dose steroid therapy, and the complete stabilization of the clinical and neuroradiological picture is observed.
